Dataproc ephemeral clusters are cheaper than always-on clusters for scheduled Spark jobs. Composer environments have baseline cost even when DAGs are idle. Exam trap: choosing streaming for a daily report or Bigtable for ad hoc analytics. The phrase "lowest cost while meeting daily SLA" usually pushes toward batch, partitioned BigQuery, and scheduled orchestration.

choose_this_when: Pub/Sub - producers are bursty, consumers are independent, replay/fan-out is needed, or the prompt says real-time events without Kafka compatibility.
Dataflow streaming - event-time processing, windows, joins, enrichment, late data, state, or sub-minute transforms are required with minimal ops.
Dataflow batch - files or tables need scalable code-based ETL and the team wants serverless execution instead of Spark cluster management.
Datastream - operational database changes must replicate continuously into BigQuery or Cloud Storage with low latency and minimal custom CDC code.
BigQuery load jobs - files can arrive in batches and the SLA is minutes to hours; lower cost and simpler than streaming writes.
BigQuery scheduled queries or Dataform - data is already in BigQuery and transformations are SQL-centric with table dependencies.
Cloud Composer - the pipeline is a multi-step DAG with schedules, dependencies, sensors, retries, backfills, and mixed services.
Workflows - a lightweight process coordinates Google APIs or HTTP services with branching/retries and does not need Airflow.
Eventarc - the trigger is an event such as object finalized in Cloud Storage, Audit Logs event, or Pub/Sub message routing to a service.
Cloud Data Fusion - the prompt says visual ETL, no-code/low-code, drag-and-drop, or business users author pipelines.
Dataproc - existing Spark, Hadoop, Hive, or custom OSS jobs must run with minimal rewrite.
Managed Service for Apache Kafka - existing Kafka clients, Kafka protocol compatibility, Kafka Streams, or Kafka Connect ecosystem is mandatory.
Bigtable - serving requires high-throughput low-latency key lookups, time-series access, or single-row reads/writes at scale.
Private Service Connect - workloads need private IP access to Google APIs or producer services without public internet paths.
VPC Service Controls - supported data services need a perimeter to reduce exfiltration from managed services.
avoid_this_when: Avoid direct device-to-BigQuery or device-to-Cloud Storage writes when bursts, retries, fan-out, or consumer backpressure are mentioned; put Pub/Sub in front.
Avoid BigQuery for sub-10 ms application serving lookups; use Bigtable, Memorystore, Spanner, or Cloud SQL based on data model.
Avoid Cloud Composer for a small event-driven API sequence; use Eventarc plus Workflows or Cloud Run.
Avoid Workflows for large Airflow-style DAGs with sensors, backfills, and many operator dependencies; use Cloud Composer.
Avoid Dataproc when the prompt says no cluster management and there is no existing Spark/Hadoop requirement; use Dataflow or BigQuery.
Avoid Dataflow when the transformation is simple SQL over BigQuery tables; use BigQuery SQL, Dataform, or scheduled queries.
Avoid Cloud Functions as the main engine for long-running streaming joins, stateful windows, or high-volume ETL; use Dataflow.
Avoid Managed Service for Apache Kafka when the only requirement is decoupled messaging on GCP; Pub/Sub is simpler.
Avoid cross-region source/process/sink placement when the prompt mentions cost, latency, or data residency.
Avoid IAM-only answers when the prompt explicitly requires customer-managed encryption keys; include Cloud KMS CMEK.
Avoid column-level access alone when raw PII must be removed or shared broadly; use Sensitive Data Protection de-identification/tokenization.
Avoid polling Cloud Storage from VMs for new files when Eventarc, Cloud Scheduler, Cloud Composer, or Storage notifications satisfy the trigger.
common_gotchas: Don't put all logic in BigQuery scheduled queries when retries/dependencies matter — use Composer.
Don't write straight to BigQuery from devices — buffer through Pub/Sub.
common_traps: Direct streaming from devices to BigQuery looks simple but fails burst buffering, retry isolation, fan-out, and client credential hygiene; the clue is many producers or unpredictable spikes.
Cloud Composer is tempting for any orchestration, but a short event-driven API sequence belongs in Workflows or Eventarc; the clue is a few service calls and no DAG ecosystem.
Workflows is tempting for orchestration, but Airflow sensors, backfills, many dependencies, and operator ecosystem mean Cloud Composer.
Dataproc is tempting for all big data processing, but "minimize operations" plus no existing Spark/Hadoop code points to Dataflow or BigQuery.
BigQuery scheduled queries are tempting for pipelines, but complex branching, non-SQL tasks, and retries across services mean Cloud Composer.
Bigtable is tempting for large data, but ad hoc SQL, joins, and BI dashboards mean BigQuery.
BigQuery is tempting for all analytics outputs, but sub-10 ms application reads by key mean Bigtable, Memorystore, Spanner, or Cloud SQL.
Kafka is tempting for event streaming, but unless the prompt requires Kafka clients or ecosystem compatibility, Pub/Sub is the lower-ops GCP answer.
Cloud Functions are tempting for every event trigger, but long-running ETL, stream joins, and high-volume transformations require Dataflow or Cloud Run plus managed services.
Adding more workers is tempting for throughput, but hot Bigtable row keys or low-cardinality Pub/Sub ordering keys require key redesign.
Firewall rules are tempting for private access, but workers without external IPs still need Private Google Access, Private Service Connect, or private Google API routing.
IAM restrictions are tempting for PII, but if raw PII must not be exposed downstream, de-identify/tokenize with Sensitive Data Protection and then enforce BigQuery controls.
CMEK on only the final BigQuery dataset is incomplete when temp buckets, Pub/Sub topics, Dataflow jobs, or raw Cloud Storage also persist sensitive data.
Cross-region pipelines look functionally correct but violate data residency, add egress cost, and increase latency; align source, process, sink, and KMS locations.
Using date-sharded BigQuery tables looks familiar but native partitioned tables are usually the exam answer for managed analytics at scale.

clue_words.phrase: bursty events, decouple producers and consumers
fan out one event stream to multiple independent consumers
poison messages or repeatedly failing events
clue_words.answer: Pub/Sub dead-letter topic plus Dataflow dead-letter sink

clue_words.phrase: event-time windows, late data, stateful stream processing
unified batch and streaming code
existing Spark or Hadoop jobs with minimal rewrite
drag-and-drop visual ETL
database change data capture into BigQuery
Kafka client compatibility
daily DAG with dependencies, retries, and backfills
sequence Google APIs and HTTP calls with retries
trigger when a Cloud Storage object is finalized
clue_words.answer: Eventarc to Workflows, Cloud Run, or Cloud Functions

clue_words.phrase: ad hoc SQL over petabyte-scale data
sub-10 ms key-value or time-series serving
globally consistent relational transactions
customer-managed encryption keys
no public IPs for pipeline workers
clue_words.answer: Private Google Access, private subnets, and private API access pattern

clue_words.phrase: private endpoint to Google APIs from VPC
reduce exfiltration from BigQuery and Cloud Storage
analysts must not see raw SSN or email
clue_words.answer: Sensitive Data Protection de-identification plus BigQuery access controls

decision_table.if_question_says: Schema must be enforced on ingest

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Real-time event ingest with independent consumers and bursty producers
Need streaming transformations, windows, state, or late-arriving events
Simple Pub/Sub messages should land in BigQuery without custom transformation
service_decision_table.answer: Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription or Dataflow only if logic is needed

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Files land in Cloud Storage and must trigger processing immediately
service_decision_table.answer: Eventarc to Workflows, Cloud Run, or Cloud Functions

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Nightly files with multiple dependent steps and retry requirements
service_decision_table.answer: Cloud Composer orchestrating BigQuery load jobs, Dataflow batch, or Dataproc

service_decision_table.if_question_says: SQL transformations already inside BigQuery with table dependencies
service_decision_table.answer: Dataform or BigQuery scheduled queries for simple schedules

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Complex DAG across BigQuery, Dataflow, Dataproc, and external systems
A few API calls with branching and retry, no Airflow operators
Operational database changes must stream into BigQuery
One-time or migration-focused database move
service_decision_table.answer: Database Migration Service, not the default ongoing CDC answer

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Existing Spark/Hadoop/Hive code must run with minimal rewrite
No-code visual ETL authoring
Kafka protocol, Kafka clients, or Kafka Connect ecosystem is required
Low-latency key-value/time-series serving at high throughput
Ad hoc BI SQL over large historical and streaming datasets
Globally consistent relational transactions at scale
Raw immutable data lake and cheap archive with lifecycle policies
No public IPs for pipeline workers but access to Google APIs is required
service_decision_table.answer: Private Google Access or Private Service Connect private API access pattern

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Multiple service projects share centrally governed networking
Customer controls encryption keys and rotation
service_decision_table.answer: Cloud KMS CMEK on each supported pipeline resource

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Reduce managed-service data exfiltration risk
Discover and mask PII before analytics
service_decision_table.answer: Sensitive Data Protection plus BigQuery governance controls

scenario_patterns.how_question_disguises_it: The prompt describes millions of mobile/web events per minute, unpredictable spikes, dashboard freshness under a minute, and downstream enrichment. Choose Pub/Sub for ingest, Dataflow streaming for validation/windowing/enrichment, BigQuery for analytic dashboards, and dead-letter handling for malformed events.

The prompt says vendors drop CSV or JSON files into a bucket every night, downstream tables must refresh by morning, and failures need retry/alerting. Choose Cloud Storage landing, BigQuery load or Dataflow batch, and Cloud Composer when dependencies and retries matter.

The prompt says the OLTP database cannot tolerate nightly full exports, analysts need changes within seconds or minutes, and deletes must be reflected. Choose Datastream CDC into BigQuery or Cloud Storage, optionally Dataflow for transformations.

The prompt emphasizes current Spark, Hive, HDFS, or Hadoop jobs and minimal code changes. Choose Dataproc, preferably ephemeral clusters for scheduled jobs, not Dataflow unless rewriting to Beam is acceptable.
The prompt says analysts or ETL developers need a visual interface and prebuilt connectors, with limited coding. Choose Cloud Data Fusion rather than hand-written Dataflow or Dataproc.

The prompt includes no external IPs, centralized networking, private access to Google APIs, and multiple service projects. Choose Shared VPC, private subnets, Private Google Access or Private Service Connect, and VPC Service Controls where data exfiltration is a concern.
The prompt says the organization must control encryption keys and rotate them periodically. Use Cloud KMS CMEK on each persistent service and compatible regional keys; grant service agents KMS roles.

The prompt describes a few sequential calls to Cloud Run, Vertex AI, BigQuery, and external HTTP endpoints with retries and branching. Choose Workflows, not Cloud Composer, unless the workflow is an Airflow-style DAG.

The prompt says users need low-latency lookup of latest device state or profile features by key. Choose Bigtable or another serving database; BigQuery can remain the analytic sink but is not the serving path.

The prompt says raw events must be retained for reprocessing and audit while curated tables support analytics. Land immutable raw data in Cloud Storage, process with Dataflow or BigQuery, and write curated BigQuery tables.

exam_pattern_notes: Latency first: sub-10 ms serving means a serving store such as Bigtable; sub-minute event analytics means Pub/Sub plus Dataflow plus BigQuery; daily SLA means batch and orchestration.
Source type first: events map to Pub/Sub, files map to Cloud Storage, database changes map to Datastream, existing Spark maps to Dataproc.
Minimize operations normally eliminates self-managed Kafka, VMs, and custom cron unless a compatibility requirement forces them.
Existing code beats theoretical fit: existing Spark/Hadoop with minimal rewrite points to Dataproc even if Dataflow would be cleaner for new code.
Kafka wording is decisive: Kafka clients, Kafka protocol, Kafka Streams, or Kafka Connect shifts the answer from Pub/Sub to Managed Service for Apache Kafka.
SQL locality matters: if data is already in BigQuery and transforms are SQL, keep processing in BigQuery with Dataform or scheduled queries.
DAG complexity matters: retries plus dependencies plus schedules across services point to Cloud Composer, not a single scheduled query.
Event trigger wording matters: object finalized, audit event, or Pub/Sub event routed to a service points to Eventarc.
Private connectivity is not just IAM: no external IPs requires private subnet and Google API access design such as Private Google Access or Private Service Connect.
CMEK wording requires Cloud KMS and compatible key locations; default encryption is not enough.
PII wording requires de-identification or tokenization when raw values must not propagate; BigQuery ACLs alone only restrict access.
Data residency wording requires aligned dataset, bucket, job, and key locations; global service names do not override data location constraints.
Replay wording favors Pub/Sub retention plus raw Cloud Storage landing; do not design a pipeline with only transient processing if audit replay is required.
Hotspot wording favors key redesign: Bigtable row-key salting/reversal or higher-cardinality Pub/Sub ordering keys, not just more compute workers.
final_review_bullets: Break questions into source, ingest, process, store, serve before choosing products.
Pub/Sub buffers bursty event producers and enables fan-out, retry, replay, and dead-letter topics.
Dataflow is managed Beam for batch plus streaming; Dataproc is for existing Spark/Hadoop.
BigQuery is analytic SQL, not millisecond serving storage.
Bigtable row keys must distribute writes; salt or reverse timestamp to avoid hot tablets.
Datastream is CDC from operational databases into BigQuery, Cloud Storage, or Cloud SQL.
Cloud Composer is Airflow DAG orchestration; Workflows is lightweight API orchestration.
Eventarc is the managed event trigger layer for Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, and Workflows.
Batch files land in Cloud Storage; load to BigQuery when SQL analytics are needed.
Use BigQuery load jobs when freshness allows; streaming costs more and is for low-latency needs.
Keep BigQuery transformations in BigQuery when SQL is enough; do not export just to transform.
CMEK key location must be compatible with the resource location.
Grant KMS key permissions to service agents, not broad human groups.
Private Google Access or private API access is needed for workers without external IPs to reach Google APIs.
Shared VPC centralizes network control across service projects.
Private Service Connect gives private IP access to Google APIs or producer services from VPCs.

2.2 Building the pipelines.what_the_exam_tests: Section 2.2 tests whether you can pick the right pipeline engine, messaging system, transformation layer, and time semantics from constraints, not whether you can define each service. Expect scenario questions that hide the answer in phrases like "existing Spark jobs," "Kafka clients cannot change," "late events arrive 10 minutes after user activity," "no cluster management," or "SQL-only analytics team." The strongest axis is managed-new-build versus existing-ecosystem migration, followed by batch versus streaming, event-time correctness, operational overhead, and sink guarantees.
must_know_services: Dataflow - Fully managed Apache Beam runner for batch and streaming pipelines with autoscaling, windowing, state, timers, and built-in GCP connectors.
Apache Beam - Unified programming model behind Dataflow; exam tests PCollections, transforms, event time, watermarks, triggers, allowed lateness, and windows.
Pub/Sub - Serverless GCP-native messaging for global event ingestion, fan-out, push or pull delivery, ordering keys, schemas, retention, retry, and dead-letter topics.
BigQuery - Analytical warehouse that can be both pipeline sink and transformation engine through SQL, scheduled queries, stored procedures, BigQuery ML, and the Storage Write API.
BigQuery Storage Write API - Preferred streaming write path into BigQuery when the question requires high throughput, lower latency, and exactly-once streams instead of legacy insertAll.
Dataproc - Managed Spark, Hadoop, Hive, Pig, Presto, Trino, HBase, and Flink clusters for lift-and-shift open source data processing.
Dataproc Serverless - Serverless Spark batch execution when Spark code is required but cluster provisioning and lifecycle management should disappear.
Cloud Data Fusion - Visual CDAP-based ETL/ELT service for no-code or low-code pipeline authoring, reusable connectors, Wrangler, and operationalized data integration.
Dataform - Version-controlled SQL workflow management for BigQuery transformations, dependencies, assertions, and CI/CD-style ELT.
Cloud Composer - Managed Apache Airflow for orchestration when the pipeline is mostly scheduling and dependency control across services, not data-parallel compute itself.
Managed Service for Apache Kafka - Google-managed Kafka-compatible clusters for workloads that require Kafka APIs, partitions, offsets, Kafka Connect, Kafka Streams, or ksqlDB.
Datastream - Serverless CDC and replication from operational databases into BigQuery, Cloud Storage, or downstream processing paths.
BigQuery Data Transfer Service - Managed scheduled ingestion from SaaS apps, Google Ads, YouTube, Cloud Storage, and other supported sources into BigQuery.
Cloud Storage - Durable raw landing zone for files, pipeline templates, staging, temporary data, and data lake inputs consumed by Dataflow, Dataproc, and BigQuery.
Data Catalog / Dataplex Universal Catalog - Metadata, discovery, governance, and lineage context around pipeline assets; usually not the compute answer but often part of operating pipelines.
Vertex AI - Model hosting and batch or online prediction target for Dataflow enrichment when inference cannot be expressed as BigQuery SQL functions.
BigQuery ML AI functions - ML.GENERATE_TEXT, ML.GENERATE_EMBEDDING, and AI.GENERATE support in-warehouse generative enrichment when data already lives in BigQuery.
Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging - Operational visibility for Dataflow job lag, throughput, worker errors, Pub/Sub backlog, Dataproc job health, and pipeline SLOs.

read_content.heading: Pipeline Service Selection
read_content.body:
Concept: PDE pipeline questions usually ask for the least-operational, most-native service that satisfies explicit constraints. Dataflow is the default for new managed batch or streaming pipelines because it runs Apache Beam without cluster operations and supports windowing, stateful transforms, autoscaling, and connectors. Dataproc is the default only when the workload is already Spark, Hadoop, Hive, HBase, Presto, Trino, or requires those libraries. Cloud Data Fusion is for visual ETL. BigQuery SQL/Dataform is for ELT after data is already in BigQuery.
Use this when: choose Dataflow for unbounded data, event-time aggregation, Pub/Sub ingestion, BigQuery/GCS sinks, and "no cluster" wording. Choose Dataproc for lift-and-shift Spark/Hadoop, custom JARs, YARN-style ecosystem needs, or HBase. Choose Dataproc Serverless when the question says Spark batch but also says no cluster management. Choose Dataform when transformations are SQL-only and must be version-controlled.
Trade-offs: Dataflow requires Beam pipeline design and has different debugging ergonomics than Spark. Dataproc gives ecosystem compatibility but still exposes cluster sizing, image versions, init actions, autoscaling policies, and idle cost unless serverless is used. Cloud Data Fusion is faster for citizen-developer integration but less flexible for complex custom code. BigQuery transforms avoid data movement but are not a general streaming engine.
Exam traps: "minimize operations" beats familiar technology unless existing code is a hard constraint. "Team knows Spark" alone is weaker than "existing Spark code must run unchanged." "Schedule SQL transformations" means Dataform or scheduled queries, not Dataflow. "Coordinate jobs" means Cloud Composer; "process records at scale" means Dataflow/Dataproc.

Dataproc is the default only when the workload is already Spark, Hadoop, Hive, HBase, Presto, Trino, or requires those libraries. Cloud Data Fusion is for visual ETL. BigQuery SQL/Dataform is for ELT after data is already in BigQuery.

Use this when: choose Dataflow for unbounded data, event-time aggregation, Pub/Sub ingestion, BigQuery/GCS sinks, and "no cluster" wording. Choose Dataproc for lift-and-shift Spark/Hadoop, custom JARs, YARN-style ecosystem needs, or HBase. Choose Dataproc Serverless when the question says Spark batch but also says no cluster management. Choose Dataform when transformations are SQL-only and must be version-controlled.

Trade-offs: Dataflow requires Beam pipeline design and has different debugging ergonomics than Spark. Dataproc gives ecosystem compatibility but still exposes cluster sizing, image versions, init actions, autoscaling policies, and idle cost unless serverless is used. Cloud Data Fusion is faster for citizen-developer integration but less flexible for complex custom code. BigQuery transforms avoid data movement but are not a general streaming engine.

Exam traps: "minimize operations" beats familiar technology unless existing code is a hard constraint. "Team knows Spark" alone is weaker than "existing Spark code must run unchanged." "Schedule SQL transformations" means Dataform or scheduled queries, not Dataflow. "Coordinate jobs" means Cloud Composer; "process records at scale" means Dataflow/Dataproc.

read_content.heading: Batch, Streaming, And Unified Beam
read_content.body:
Concept: Batch processes bounded data; streaming processes unbounded data and must reason about time, ordering, retries, and late arrivals. Apache Beam gives one programming model for both through PCollections and transforms; Dataflow is Google Cloud's managed Beam runner. The exam often describes a stream but hides the real test in whether aggregations are based on event time or processing time.
Use this when: Dataflow streaming fits Pub/Sub-to-BigQuery pipelines, real-time dashboards, fraud signals, IoT events, clickstream sessionization, and enrichment that must run continuously. Dataflow batch fits large-scale file conversion, joins, reshaping, and backfills without provisioning Spark clusters. BigQuery batch SQL fits set-based warehouse transforms. Dataproc batch fits Spark jobs that already exist or need Spark libraries.
Trade-offs: Streaming has higher operational complexity than batch because windows, watermarks, triggers, state TTL, checkpointing, and sink idempotency affect correctness. Batch is easier to replay and cheaper to reason about, but it cannot meet low-latency requirements. Micro-batch Spark can process frequent batches, but Dataflow is the exam's preferred answer for true unbounded event streams with windowing.
Exam traps: A phrase like "data arrives continuously" is not enough; look for the required output latency. "Hourly reports" can be batch even if source events are continuous. "Less than one minute" or "near real time" usually pushes to Pub/Sub plus Dataflow streaming. "Exactly-once" is not guaranteed by simply saying streaming; source, transforms, and sink must support idempotent or exactly-once semantics.

The exam often describes a stream but hides the real test in whether aggregations are based on event time or processing time.

read_content.heading: Apache Beam Core Model
read_content.body:
Concept: Beam pipelines are DAGs of transforms over PCollections. A PCollection may be bounded or unbounded; transforms include ParDo, MapElements, GroupByKey, Combine, CoGroupByKey, Flatten, Partition, and IO connectors. In streaming, every GroupByKey or aggregation must have a windowing strategy unless using the global window with a trigger. Beam separates event time from processing time, which is the foundation for correct stream aggregation.
Use this when: answer Beam concepts when the question asks how to handle out-of-order events, late data, user sessions, rolling metrics, deduplication by event id, side inputs, per-key state, or timers. Use ParDo for row-level custom logic. Use Combine instead of GroupByKey when you can aggregate locally and reduce shuffle. Use side inputs for small reference data. Use state and timers for per-key custom session logic or dedupe horizons.
Trade-offs: Beam portability is useful, but the PDE exam focuses on Dataflow behavior. GroupByKey and joins create shuffle and can expose hot keys. Side inputs are convenient but must fit memory and refresh semantics; large reference datasets belong in BigQuery, Bigtable, Spanner, or an external lookup pattern, not as a huge side input in a streaming job.
Exam traps: Do not use processing-time windows for business events that carry event timestamps. Do not use a single global GroupByKey on an unbounded stream without triggers. Do not assume input order from Pub/Sub becomes output order after shuffle. Ordering keys preserve per-key delivery ordering at subscription delivery, not total ordering through a parallel pipeline.

read_content.heading: Windows, Watermarks, And Triggers
read_content.body:
Concept: Windows split event-time data into finite groups. Fixed windows create non-overlapping intervals, sliding windows create overlapping intervals for moving metrics, session windows merge events separated by less than an inactivity gap, and the global window groups everything. A watermark is Dataflow's estimate that events up to time T have likely arrived. The default trigger fires when the watermark passes the end of the window; custom triggers can emit early speculative panes or late correction panes.
Use this when: fixed windows fit "count per minute" or "hourly revenue." Sliding windows fit "last 5 minutes updated every 1 minute." Session windows fit "user session ends after 30 minutes inactivity." Global windows fit bounded batch or special streaming cases with explicit triggers. Allowed lateness keeps a window open after the watermark passes so late events can update prior results.
Trade-offs: Early triggers reduce latency but produce provisional results that downstream consumers must tolerate. Late triggers improve correctness but can create updates, duplicates, or retractions depending on accumulation mode and sink behavior. Longer allowed lateness increases state retention and cost. Short lateness improves cost and finality but drops more valid events.
Exam traps: The exam often says "mobile devices go offline and upload later" or "events can arrive 10 minutes late." The answer is event time plus allowed lateness, not processing time. "Need a dashboard every 30 seconds before the window closes" points to early triggers. "Final accurate result after two hours" points to allowed lateness plus late panes or a batch correction path.

read_content.heading: Late Data, Deduplication, And Idempotency
read_content.body:
Concept: Late data is data whose event time belongs to a window for which the watermark has already advanced. Beam's default allowed lateness is 0, so late data may be dropped unless configured. Deduplication requires a stable event id and a retention horizon. Idempotency means retries or duplicate delivery do not change the final result more than once.
Use this when: set allowed lateness when the scenario states a bounded delay such as "up to 15 minutes late." Route data later than the allowed horizon to a side output, Pub/Sub dead-letter topic, Cloud Storage quarantine path, or BigQuery error table for audit and replay. Use event ids to dedupe at ingestion or in Dataflow state. Use deterministic keys and upserts when writing to mutable external systems.
Trade-offs: Exactly-once is end-to-end only if the source, transforms, side effects, and sink cooperate. A custom DoFn that sends email, increments an external counter, or calls a non-idempotent API can run more than once during retry. BigQuery Storage Write API can provide stronger streaming semantics than legacy streaming inserts, but your pipeline still needs deterministic records and appropriate stream mode.
Exam traps: "Dataflow provides exactly-once" is not enough if a custom side effect is present. "Retry failed calls" is unsafe without idempotency keys. "Drop malformed records" is rarely correct; the exam prefers dead-letter or quarantine paths. "Use Pub/Sub ordering" does not dedupe; ordering and duplicate handling are separate concerns.

Deduplication requires a stable event id and a retention horizon. Idempotency means retries or duplicate delivery do not change the final result more than once.

Use this when: set allowed lateness when the scenario states a bounded delay such as "up to 15 minutes late." Route data later than the allowed horizon to a side output, Pub/Sub dead-letter topic, Cloud Storage quarantine path, or BigQuery error table for audit and replay. Use event ids to dedupe at ingestion or in Dataflow state. Use deterministic keys and upserts when writing to mutable external systems.

Trade-offs: Exactly-once is end-to-end only if the source, transforms, side effects, and sink cooperate. A custom DoFn that sends email, increments an external counter, or calls a non-idempotent API can run more than once during retry. BigQuery Storage Write API can provide stronger streaming semantics than legacy streaming inserts, but your pipeline still needs deterministic records and appropriate stream mode.

Exam traps: "Dataflow provides exactly-once" is not enough if a custom side effect is present. "Retry failed calls" is unsafe without idempotency keys. "Drop malformed records" is rarely correct; the exam prefers dead-letter or quarantine paths. "Use Pub/Sub ordering" does not dedupe; ordering and duplicate handling are separate concerns.

read_content.heading: Pub/Sub Ingestion Patterns
read_content.body:
Concept: Pub/Sub is the standard GCP-native ingestion buffer for event streams. Topics receive messages; subscriptions deliver messages to consumers by pull or push. It supports at-least-once delivery by default, message retention, retry policies, dead-letter topics, schemas, filters, snapshots, seek, and ordering keys. Topic and subscription message retention can be configured up to 31 days; unacknowledged messages are retained for retry until retention expires.
Use this when: choose Pub/Sub for decoupling producers from consumers, fan-out, burst absorption, global event ingestion, Cloud Run or Cloud Functions triggers, and Dataflow streaming sources. Use pull subscriptions for high-throughput workers and Dataflow. Use push for HTTPS endpoints that can handle request authentication and backpressure. Use subscription filters when consumers need only a subset of attributes.
Trade-offs: Pub/Sub is not Kafka. It has topics and subscriptions rather than partitions and consumer offsets exposed like Kafka. Ordering keys provide ordered delivery per key only when enabled and when publishers use the same key. Exactly-once delivery in Pub/Sub applies to pull subscriptions in supported configurations and does not remove the need for idempotent downstream processing.
Exam traps: "Strict order for all messages" is a red flag; Pub/Sub can order per ordering key, not globally at high scale. "Poison message blocks progress" points to a dead-letter topic and max delivery attempts. "Malformed payloads should be rejected before publishing" points to Pub/Sub schemas using Avro or Protocol Buffers. "Multiple independent consumers" means multiple subscriptions, not multiple readers sharing one subscription unless they are competing consumers.

read_content.heading: Pub/Sub vs Managed Service For Apache Kafka
read_content.body:
Concept: Pub/Sub is serverless, GCP-native messaging. Managed Service for Apache Kafka is for Kafka-compatible workloads that require Kafka protocol, partitions, offsets, Kafka Connect, Kafka Streams, ksqlDB, or existing client compatibility. The decision is usually not "which stream service is better" but "can the application change its messaging contract?"
Use this when: choose Pub/Sub when building a new Google Cloud pipeline, when global serverless ingestion is desired, when Dataflow is the main consumer, or when operational simplicity dominates. Choose Managed Service for Apache Kafka when the question says Kafka API, Kafka Connect connectors, consumer groups with offsets, partition semantics, existing Kafka clients, Kafka Streams applications, or migration without code rewrite.
Trade-offs: Kafka gives ecosystem compatibility and partition/offset semantics but is still a cluster-oriented streaming platform, even when managed. Pub/Sub removes most broker operations and integrates naturally with IAM, Dataflow, Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, and BigQuery subscriptions. Pub/Sub Lite historically appeared as a cost-optimized zonal/regional log option, but for Kafka migrations the exam now favors Managed Service for Apache Kafka when Kafka compatibility is explicit.
Exam traps: "Kafka-compatible" does not mean Pub/Sub. "Minimize code changes from on-prem Kafka" means Managed Service for Apache Kafka. "Need serverless global event bus" means Pub/Sub. "Need ordering for a user_id" can be Pub/Sub ordering keys; "need Kafka partition offsets" is Kafka.

read_content.heading: Dataflow Operational Controls
read_content.body:
Concept: Dataflow abstracts workers but still has operational knobs: worker region/zone, service account, network, autoscaling, max workers, Streaming Engine, Dataflow Prime, drain, cancel, update, snapshots, logs, and metrics. Streaming Engine moves shuffle and state off worker VMs for streaming jobs and is commonly the right answer for autoscaling, reduced worker resource pressure, and lower operational burden.
Use this when: use drain for graceful streaming shutdown or replacement because it stops reading new input and finishes in-flight work. Use cancel when immediate stop is acceptable and data loss or replay is handled. Use job update for compatible pipeline graph changes that preserve state. Use snapshots to save streaming pipeline state for recovery or migration where supported. Set max workers and autoscaling to control cost.
Trade-offs: Autoscaling can reduce cost but may not fix a hot key, slow external API, or downstream quota bottleneck. Vertical autoscaling and right fitting in Dataflow Prime help memory-bound stages, but poor key distribution still needs design changes. Flex Templates package dependencies in a container and improve reproducibility; classic templates are simpler when parameters are enough.
Exam traps: "Deploy a new version without losing in-flight data" points to drain or update, not cancel. "One worker is lagging while others idle" points to hot key mitigation, not simply adding workers. "Custom native dependencies" points to Flex Templates. "No SDK installed for operators" points to templates.

read_content.heading: Dataflow Performance And Hot Keys
read_content.body:
Concept: Dataflow performance failures usually come from shuffle, hot keys, oversized side inputs, slow external calls, or downstream sinks. A hot key is a key with disproportionate records, causing one worker or shard to lag. Metrics such as system lag, data freshness, backlog, elements per second, and per-step errors reveal whether the bottleneck is input, transform, or sink.
Use this when: use Combine.perKey before GroupByKey where possible, fanout or salting for hot keys, batching for external API calls, asynchronous IO where available, and side outputs for bad records. For Bigtable sinks, design row keys to distribute writes; salting or reversing timestamp prefixes may avoid sequential hot spots. For BigQuery sinks, use Storage Write API and monitor rejected rows or quota errors.
Trade-offs: Salting spreads load but complicates reads and requires a second aggregation if exact per-key totals are needed. More workers increase parallelism only when the graph has parallelizable work. Larger machines help memory-heavy transforms but do not fix skew. Calling remote services inline can dominate latency and needs rate limits, retries with backoff, and idempotency.
Exam traps: "Pipeline falls behind only for a small number of customers" means hot keys. "Increase worker count" is a distractor when key skew is the root cause. "Load full reference table as side input" is wrong if the table is large or changes often. "Use Bigtable with timestamp-leading row keys" can create hotspots for time-ordered writes.

read_content.heading: BigQuery As Pipeline Sink And Transform Engine
read_content.body:
Concept: BigQuery appears in pipeline questions as a sink, source, and transformation engine. For ingestion, Dataflow can write to BigQuery using BigQueryIO, and the Storage Write API is the modern streaming path. For transformations, scheduled queries, stored procedures, BigQuery scripting, materialized views, and Dataform handle SQL-first ELT. BigQuery is not a general record-by-record orchestration engine.
Use this when: choose BigQuery SQL when data already lands in BigQuery and transformations are relational: joins, aggregations, dedupe, slowly changing dimensions, assertions, and derived tables. Choose Dataform when dependencies, Git versioning, environments, testing assertions, and deployment workflow matter. Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service when ingestion is from supported SaaS or Google products and should be managed.
Trade-offs: ELT in BigQuery minimizes data movement and uses warehouse scale, but costs are query-slot driven and transformations are batch or scheduled unless using continuous ingestion plus downstream queries. Dataflow is better for per-event enrichment before landing, event-time windows, Pub/Sub sources, and custom IO. Dataproc is better for Spark-specific libraries and file formats when code already exists.
Exam traps: "Use Dataflow to run SQL transformations already in BigQuery" is often overengineering. "Version-controlled SQL DAG" is Dataform, not Cloud Composer alone. "Ingest Google Ads into BigQuery on a schedule" is BigQuery Data Transfer Service, not a custom pipeline. "Exactly-once streaming into BigQuery" should make you look for Storage Write API rather than legacy streaming inserts.

read_content.heading: Dataproc And Spark Ecosystem
read_content.body:
Concept: Dataproc provides managed clusters for open source engines: Spark, Hadoop MapReduce, Hive, Pig, HBase, Presto, Trino, Flink, and related tooling. It is the exam answer for compatibility, not for lowest operations. Clusters should be ephemeral for batch jobs, with data in Cloud Storage and metadata in Dataproc Metastore or BigQuery rather than on local HDFS.
Use this when: choose Dataproc for lift-and-shift Hadoop/Spark, custom Spark libraries, Hive jobs, HBase workloads, or when the organization requires open source APIs. Use initialization actions for cluster customization. Use autoscaling policies and secondary workers for cost-sensitive batch. Use Spot VMs only for fault-tolerant batch workers, not masters or latency-critical processing. Use Dataproc Serverless for Spark batch when no cluster lifecycle should be managed.
Trade-offs: Dataproc clusters give control over versions, machine types, local SSDs, network, and components, but require tuning and lifecycle management. Persistent clusters are useful for interactive shared environments, but daily ETL should generally use ephemeral clusters or serverless jobs. Cloud Storage is durable and cheap, but some Spark workloads need connector tuning and partition-aware layout to avoid small-file problems.
Exam traps: "Keep a 24/7 cluster for a daily two-hour job" is usually wrong. "Move data into HDFS for durability" is wrong on Google Cloud; use Cloud Storage. "No ops but Spark required" means Dataproc Serverless, not Dataflow unless rewriting Beam is allowed. "Existing Hive metastore" points to Dataproc Metastore, not hand-maintained metadata.

read_content.heading: Cloud Data Fusion And Visual ETL
read_content.body:
Concept: Cloud Data Fusion is a fully managed, visual data integration service based on CDAP. It lets teams build ETL/ELT pipelines with plugins, Wrangler transformations, reusable connections, and a graphical canvas. It often runs execution on Dataproc behind the scenes, but the exam clue is the authoring experience rather than the runtime.
Use this when: choose Cloud Data Fusion when analysts or data engineers need no-code or low-code pipeline design, reusable connectors, visual wrangling, and operationalization without writing Beam or Spark. It fits batch integration between databases, SaaS sources, Cloud Storage, and BigQuery where the organization prioritizes fast visual development and maintainability by non-developers.
Trade-offs: Cloud Data Fusion is less suitable for advanced streaming semantics, highly custom code paths, specialized Beam state/timer logic, or ultra-low-latency event processing. It can simplify pipeline construction but still needs governance, environment management, plugin compatibility, and runtime cost awareness. If the requirement is simply orchestrating existing jobs, Cloud Composer is a better fit.
Exam traps: "No-code visual ETL" is Cloud Data Fusion. "Drag-and-drop transformation" is Cloud Data Fusion. "Serverless event-time streaming with late data" remains Dataflow. "SQL transformations with Git" is Dataform. "Schedule and monitor dependencies across systems" is Cloud Composer.

read_content.heading: Orchestration vs Processing
read_content.body:
Concept: Orchestration controls when and in what order work runs; processing performs the data transformation. Cloud Composer is managed Apache Airflow and should coordinate tasks such as Dataproc jobs, Dataflow templates, BigQuery queries, Cloud Run tasks, and notifications. It is not the service that directly performs large distributed joins or stream aggregations.
Use this when: choose Cloud Composer when the question says DAG, dependencies, retries, schedule, backfill, SLA monitoring, or coordination across services. Use Workflows for simpler service-to-service orchestration without Airflow ecosystem needs. Use Cloud Scheduler for simple time-based triggers. Use Eventarc for event-driven triggers from Cloud Storage or audit events.
Trade-offs: Composer adds Airflow operational concepts: DAG parsing, workers, scheduler, environment versioning, and dependency management. It is appropriate when orchestration complexity justifies it. For a single scheduled BigQuery SQL transformation, Dataform or scheduled queries is simpler. For streaming, orchestration starts or updates jobs; it does not replace Pub/Sub/Dataflow.
Exam traps: "Run this transformation every hour" does not automatically mean Composer; if the transformation is SQL in BigQuery, scheduled queries or Dataform may be enough. "Complex dependency graph across Dataflow, Dataproc, and BigQuery" means Composer. "Process each incoming Pub/Sub message" means Dataflow, Cloud Run, or Cloud Functions depending on complexity, not Composer.

"Process each incoming Pub/Sub message" means Dataflow, Cloud Run, or Cloud Functions depending on complexity, not Composer.

read_content.heading: Integrating New Data Sources
read_content.body:
Concept: Pipeline architecture starts by matching source type to ingestion pattern. Files land in Cloud Storage, operational database changes use Datastream or database-native export, SaaS transfers use BigQuery Data Transfer Service when supported, custom event producers publish to Pub/Sub, and Kafka producers may use Managed Service for Apache Kafka if compatibility is required. Raw landing before transformation supports replay and audit.
Use this when: use Cloud Storage plus Eventarc or notifications for file-triggered processing. Use Datastream for change data capture from supported relational databases into BigQuery or Cloud Storage-backed pipelines. Use Pub/Sub for custom application events. Use Transfer Service or Storage Transfer Service for bulk file movement, depending on source. Use BigQuery external or load jobs for batch files when latency can be minutes or hours.
Trade-offs: Managed connectors reduce code and operations but may constrain transformations and freshness. Custom ingestion offers flexibility but increases maintenance, retries, auth, schema handling, and observability responsibilities. CDC captures row changes but requires ordering, deletes, schema evolution, and merge logic. Landing raw data increases storage cost but enables replay after pipeline bugs.
Exam traps: "Supported SaaS to BigQuery" points to BigQuery Data Transfer Service, not Dataflow. "Database changes in near real time" points to Datastream plus downstream processing. "Upload CSVs nightly" can be Cloud Storage plus BigQuery load job, not streaming. "Never lose source records" means land immutable raw data before destructive transforms.

Raw landing before transformation supports replay and audit.

read_content.heading: Schema, Bad Records, And DLQ Design
read_content.body:
Concept: Production pipelines need schema enforcement and bad-record isolation. Pub/Sub schemas can validate Avro or Protocol Buffer messages at publish time. Dataflow can use side outputs for failed parses, validation errors, or external API failures. BigQuery load jobs and Storage Write API expose error behavior differently; the correct exam answer usually preserves rejected data for replay.
Use this when: enforce schemas at the earliest practical boundary. Use Pub/Sub schemas when producers publish structured events. Use BigQuery schema evolution carefully for nullable additions and compatible changes. Use dead-letter topics for Pub/Sub redelivery failures. Use BigQuery error tables or Cloud Storage quarantine buckets for invalid records. Include error reason, original payload, event id, and pipeline version in the DLQ record.
Trade-offs: Strict schemas prevent bad data but can break producers during schema changes. Permissive ingestion keeps pipelines moving but shifts quality work downstream. DLQs add operational work because someone must monitor, alert, fix, and replay. Allowing unknown values or max bad records may be acceptable for batch loads but can hide upstream contract violations if not monitored.
Exam traps: "Skip bad records" is weak unless paired with audit and replay. "Set maxBadRecords high" is not a governance strategy. "Malformed messages should never reach subscribers" means Pub/Sub schema validation. "Pipeline should continue while retaining failed records" means side output or DLQ, not job failure.

read_content.heading: AI Enrichment In Pipelines
read_content.body:
Concept: AI enrichment appears as classification, extraction, summarization, embeddings, or entity detection inside a data pipeline. If data is already in BigQuery and the operation is prompt-per-row or embedding-per-row, BigQuery ML functions such as ML.GENERATE_TEXT, ML.GENERATE_EMBEDDING, and AI.GENERATE are often the simplest answer. If enrichment must happen in a streaming pipeline or with custom pre/post-processing, Dataflow can call a Vertex AI endpoint.
Use this when: choose BigQuery AI functions for SQL-native enrichment over warehouse tables, batch prompt execution, embedding generation for VECTOR_SEARCH, or structured output extraction where governance favors staying in BigQuery. Choose Dataflow plus Vertex AI for Pub/Sub streaming enrichment, custom model endpoints, online predictions, throttled API calls, or enrichment before writing to sinks.
Trade-offs: LLM calls add latency, cost, quotas, and nondeterminism. Pipelines should batch where supported, cache deterministic prompts, use idempotency keys, and handle retryable versus permanent failures separately. For sensitive fields, combine with DLP/de-identification, policy tags, row access, or separate protected datasets as required.
Exam traps: "Classify each row already in BigQuery" is not a custom Python pipeline by default. "Low-latency stream enrichment" is not a scheduled BigQuery query. "Guarantee identical output from a generative model" is suspect; use deterministic settings where available and store model/version/prompt metadata. "PII plus AI" needs governance, not just model invocation.

Use this when: choose BigQuery AI functions for SQL-native enrichment over warehouse tables, batch prompt execution, embedding generation for VECTOR_SEARCH, or structured output extraction where governance favors staying in BigQuery. Choose Dataflow plus Vertex AI for Pub/Sub streaming enrichment, custom model endpoints, online predictions, throttled API calls, or enrichment before writing to sinks.

Trade-offs: LLM calls add latency, cost, quotas, and nondeterminism. Pipelines should batch where supported, cache deterministic prompts, use idempotency keys, and handle retryable versus permanent failures separately. For sensitive fields, combine with DLP/de-identification, policy tags, row access, or separate protected datasets as required.

Exam traps: "Classify each row already in BigQuery" is not a custom Python pipeline by default. "Low-latency stream enrichment" is not a scheduled BigQuery query. "Guarantee identical output from a generative model" is suspect; use deterministic settings where available and store model/version/prompt metadata. "PII plus AI" needs governance, not just model invocation.

read_content.heading: Security, Networking, And Governance In Pipelines
read_content.body:
Concept: Pipeline construction questions often include security constraints that change the service configuration, not the core service choice. Dataflow and Dataproc jobs run as service accounts and need least-privilege IAM on Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage, BigQuery, KMS keys, networks, and external endpoints. Private IP, VPC Service Controls, CMEK, Secret Manager, and DLP can be decisive details.
Use this when: use service-account-per-pipeline for blast-radius control. Use Secret Manager for database passwords or API keys. Use CMEK when the question requires customer-managed encryption keys. Use VPC Service Controls to reduce data exfiltration risk around BigQuery, Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, and Dataflow-supported services. Use Sensitive Data Protection for discovery, inspection, masking, tokenization, or de-identification of PII before broad access.
Trade-offs: More security controls increase deployment complexity and can break pipelines if service agents lack key permissions, subnet access, or perimeter rules. Private workers need routes/NAT or Private Service Connect for external access patterns. CMEK-protected resources require key availability and correct IAM for service agents.
Exam traps: "Encrypt with your own keys" means CMEK, not just default encryption. "Do not expose workers to public internet" means private IP workers and controlled egress. "Mask PII before analysts query BigQuery" means Sensitive Data Protection or BigQuery policy controls depending on whether data must be transformed or access-limited only. "Least privilege" means custom service accounts, not broad Owner roles.

read_content.heading: Observability And Troubleshooting
read_content.body:
Concept: Building pipelines includes operating them. Dataflow exposes job graph stages, worker logs, sampled exceptions, throughput, backlog, system lag, and data freshness. Pub/Sub exposes oldest unacked message age, backlog bytes/messages, ack latency, and delivery attempts. Dataproc exposes job driver output, cluster logs, YARN/Spark UI, and component metrics. BigQuery exposes job history, slot use, errors, and INFORMATION_SCHEMA views.
Use this when: identify whether failures are source backlog, transform errors, hot keys, sink quotas, or external dependency latency. Use Cloud Monitoring alerts for Pub/Sub backlog and Dataflow data freshness. Use Cloud Logging filtered by job_id or pipeline labels. Use Error Reporting for application exceptions where applicable. Use labels on jobs and resources for cost attribution and incident triage.
Trade-offs: Retries hide transient failures but can amplify poison records or non-idempotent side effects. More logging helps debugging but can expose sensitive data and add cost. Sampling is useful for high-volume pipelines, but DLQ records need enough original context to replay. Dashboards must reflect business freshness, not just worker CPU.
Exam traps: "CPU is low but lag grows" suggests downstream bottleneck, hot key, or blocked IO, not more CPU. "Only one stage is red in Dataflow graph" points to that transform or sink. "Messages pile up in Pub/Sub" can be slow subscribers, ack deadline issues, or Dataflow lag. "Need audit of failed rows" means DLQ/error table plus monitoring.
