choose_this_when: Choose Looker when the prompt says governed metrics, semantic layer, LookML, reusable dimensions/measures, embedded analytics, row-level access by user attribute, or one source of truth across departments.
Choose Looker Studio when the prompt says free, lightweight, ad hoc, quick dashboard, simple sharing, or low governance needs over already-curated data.
Choose Connected Sheets when analysts need pivot tables, formulas, and charts in Google Sheets over live BigQuery data without CSV exports or local copies.
Choose BI Engine when repeated interactive dashboards need sub-second or very low latency and hot data can fit in a regional in-memory reservation.
Choose BigQuery materialized views when many dashboards repeat eligible aggregates, filters, pre-joins, or small-result queries over large base tables.
Choose partitioned tables plus require_partition_filter when dashboards should always restrict time and must avoid full historical scans.
Choose clustering when dashboard filters or joins repeatedly use high-cardinality columns inside selected partitions.
Choose authorized views when users should query a safe row/column projection without direct access to the base table.
Choose policy tags plus dynamic data masking when users may query a column but should see masked PII unless they have fine-grained access.
Choose Sensitive Data Protection when the requirement says discover, classify, tokenize, anonymize, or de-identify PII before analytics.
Choose Dataform when BigQuery SQL transformations for dashboard tables need versioned dependencies, assertions, and scheduled builds.
Choose Pub/Sub plus Dataflow plus BigQuery when dashboard data freshness must be seconds-to-minutes from streaming events.
Choose maximum_bytes_billed or custom quotas when ad hoc users or Connected Sheets refreshes could accidentally scan too much data.
avoid_this_when: Avoid Looker Studio when the requirement says governed enterprise semantic layer, consistent KPIs across teams, complex access filters, or embedded customer analytics.
Avoid Connected Sheets when the requirement is centrally governed dashboards for many viewers, sub-second BI, or embedded analytics inside an application.
Avoid BI Engine as the only fix when queries scan unpartitioned TB-scale tables, have poor joins, or use unsupported SQL patterns that fall back to BigQuery.
Avoid materialized views for one-off exploratory SQL, highly variable queries, unsupported constructs, or transformations needing complex procedural orchestration.
Avoid normal logical views as a performance answer; they centralize SQL but do not store results.
Avoid dashboard-level filters as a security boundary for PII, tenant isolation, or regulatory row restrictions.
Avoid exporting BigQuery data to CSV or unmanaged spreadsheets when the prompt says live, governed, auditable, or single source of truth.
Avoid Cloud SQL, Firestore, or Bigtable for ad hoc SQL visualization over large analytical datasets.
Avoid Cloud Composer alone for streaming dashboards with seconds-level freshness; it orchestrates jobs but is not a streaming transform engine.
Avoid buying more slots before checking partition pruning, bytes scanned, query plan, materialized views, and BI Engine acceleration.
Avoid granting users BigQuery Data Viewer on base tables if the design relies on authorized views to hide sensitive rows or columns.
Avoid column-level access control alone when the requirement says de-identify, anonymize, tokenize, or mask values by role.
common_traps: Trap: Choosing Looker Studio when the prompt says governed metrics or single source of truth. Giveaway phrase: different teams calculate the same KPI differently.
Trap: Choosing Looker for a one-off simple internal dashboard. Giveaway phrase: quick free ad hoc report with no governance requirement.
Trap: Exporting BigQuery to CSV or local Sheets for visualization. Giveaway phrase: live data, auditable access, billions of rows, or no copies allowed.
Trap: Treating BI Engine as a fix for bad table design. Giveaway phrase: dashboard scans entire unpartitioned history or lacks date filters.
Trap: Treating a logical view as a performance optimization. Giveaway phrase: create a view so dashboards run faster; standard views do not store results.
Trap: Using query cache for predictable latency. Giveaway phrase: must guarantee sub-second or consistent performance under changing filters.
Trap: Granting base table access with an authorized view. Giveaway phrase: users can still query sensitive columns directly.
Trap: Hiding a PII field in the BI report instead of enforcing BigQuery security. Giveaway phrase: should not be accessible even if users query the dataset.
Trap: Using policy tags when the requirement says anonymize or tokenize. Giveaway phrase: remove raw identifiers before analysts see data.
Trap: Choosing Cloud Composer for continuous streaming transformation. Giveaway phrase: update dashboard within seconds of each event.
Trap: Choosing Bigtable for BI because of fast lookups. Giveaway phrase: analysts need SQL joins, ad hoc slicing, and dashboards over large datasets.
Trap: Adding slots before diagnosing query shape. Giveaway phrase: high bytes processed from full scans rather than queueing or slot contention.
Trap: Creating many dashboard-specific calculated fields. Giveaway phrase: metric drift or inconsistent definitions across reports.
Trap: Search index for aggregate dashboard slowness. Giveaway phrase: slow revenue by day or count by region; use pre-aggregation, partitioning, clustering, and BI Engine.
Trap: Connected Sheets as a governance layer. Giveaway phrase: enforce organization-wide metric definitions and access policies.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Restrict rows by tenant, region, or department

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Find and de-identify sensitive data before visualization
Need versioned BigQuery SQL models with tests and dependencies

service_decision_table.answer: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT, query plan, BI Engine metrics, and Cloud Monitoring

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need low-latency transactional app UI, not BI
service_decision_table.answer: Cloud SQL, Spanner, Firestore, or Bigtable depending on transactional pattern; not BigQuery dashboard design

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need SQL analytics and BI over petabyte-scale warehouse
service_decision_table.answer: BigQuery, not Bigtable or Cloud SQL

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need customer-facing embedded analytics with governed metrics

scenario_patterns.how_question_disguises_it: Several departments have dashboards with different revenue, active user, or churn definitions. The question mentions reusable business definitions, governed metrics, a single source of truth, access rules, and self-service exploration. Pick Looker with LookML or centralized semantic modeling, not Looker Studio calculated fields or spreadsheet exports.

A small team wants a fast, low-cost dashboard over curated BigQuery tables with no strong semantic layer or embedded analytics requirement. The wording says quick, free, simple sharing, marketing report, or prototype. Pick Looker Studio, while leaving performance and security controls in BigQuery.

Business analysts live in Sheets and need pivot tables or formulas on BigQuery data that is too large for a normal spreadsheet. The prompt says familiar spreadsheet, no SQL, avoid CSV exports, and live BigQuery source. Pick Connected Sheets.

An executive dashboard runs the same aggregate queries for many viewers and must load interactively. The prompt includes repeated refreshes, high concurrency, expensive GROUP BY, or sub-second expectations. Use materialized views or aggregate tables for precomputation and BI Engine for in-memory acceleration.

Dashboards filter by date in the UI but generated SQL still scans years of events, or analysts forget date filters. The giveaway is high bytes processed and time-series tables. Partition on the date column, require partition filters, cluster secondary predicates, and cap ad hoc bytes.

A dashboard needs broad access but contains SSN, email, salary, health, or tenant-sensitive data. The question asks for masking by role, safe sharing, or least privilege. Use policy tags, dynamic data masking, row access policies, authorized views, and avoid granting base table access.

scenario_patterns.pattern: PII must be transformed, not just hidden
scenario_patterns.how_question_disguises_it: The prompt says anonymize, tokenize, de-identify, discover PII, or share data with analysts who should not receive raw identifiers. This is Sensitive Data Protection plus governed BigQuery tables. Column hiding in a dashboard is insufficient.

The dashboard monitors clicks, transactions, IoT, fraud, logs, or operational events with seconds-to-minutes freshness. The answer is Pub/Sub for ingestion, Dataflow streaming for transformation, BigQuery for analytical serving, and BI design for read latency.

The report refreshes daily or hourly and must be reproducible, tested, and version controlled. The question mentions SQL transformations, dependencies, assertions, and BigQuery-native models. Pick Dataform or scheduled BigQuery pipelines, not streaming services.

A large BigQuery log or transaction table backs dashboards where users search by request ID, transaction ID, user ID, or text. The correct accelerator is a BigQuery search index for selective lookup predicates, not a materialized aggregate view.

exam_pattern_notes: Mentions PII and BigQuery dashboards together -> enforce BigQuery data-layer controls; use policy tags, dynamic data masking, row access policies, authorized views, and Sensitive Data Protection as required by wording.
Mentions anonymize, tokenize, de-identify, or discover sensitive fields -> Sensitive Data Protection; policy tags alone only govern access or masking.
Mentions single source of truth, governed metrics, or departments disagreeing about KPIs -> Looker with LookML or centralized semantic model, not Looker Studio alone.
Mentions free, quick, ad hoc dashboard with minimal governance -> Looker Studio is usually correct.
Mentions spreadsheet, pivot table, finance analyst, no SQL, and live BigQuery -> Connected Sheets, not export to CSV.
Mentions sub-second interactive dashboard -> BI Engine, usually after partitioning, clustering, and pre-aggregation are correct.
Mentions repeated aggregate queries over large tables -> BigQuery materialized views or aggregate tables; logical views are a distractor.
Mentions slow because bytes processed are high -> fix partition pruning, clustering, SELECT list, joins, materialized views, and query shape before buying more slots.
Mentions slow because queries are queued or slot_ms contention is high after efficient SQL -> consider reservations, editions, or capacity management.
Mentions dashboard scans all history -> require_partition_filter plus date partitioning is the exam clue.
Mentions high-cardinality lookup by ID or text in BigQuery -> search index; not BI Engine alone and not materialized aggregate view.
Mentions streaming freshness -> Pub/Sub and Dataflow feed BigQuery; BI Engine only accelerates reads after data is available.
Mentions daily or hourly report refresh -> batch SQL/Dataform/scheduled queries are simpler than streaming.

Mentions row-level tenant isolation -> row access policies or Looker access filters; dashboard filters are not a security boundary.Black-box BI slowness question -> identify generated BigQuery job first with INFORMATION_SCHEMA before redesigning.No unmanaged copies constraint -> avoid CSV exports and duplicated Sheets;
final_review_bullets: Looker = governed semantic BI with LookML and reusable metrics.
Looker Studio = quick self-service dashboards, not strong metric governance.
Connected Sheets = spreadsheet interface over live BigQuery, not CSV export.
BI Engine = in-memory acceleration for repeated interactive BigQuery dashboards.
BI Engine reservation is regional and tied to the billing/query project; default max is commonly 250 GiB per project per location.
Materialized view = stored precomputed result; logical view = stored SQL only.
Materialized views and BI Engine are often paired: precompute plus memory acceleration.
Partition first by dashboard time filter; cluster by common filter, join, and group columns.
require_partition_filter prevents dashboards and analysts from scanning all history accidentally.
maximum_bytes_billed caps query blast radius but can break overly broad reports.
Authorized views expose safe projections without base-table access.
Policy tags control sensitive columns; dynamic data masking changes returned values by permission.
Row access policies filter rows; policy tags mask or restrict columns.
Sensitive Data Protection discovers and de-identifies PII before broad analytics.
Search indexes help selective ID/text lookup filters, not aggregate rollups.

Query cache is not a predictable dashboard architecture; use BI Engine or materialized views for repeatability..JOBS_BY_PROJECT and query plan are first stops for slow dashboard triage.Dataform fits versioned SQL models and assertions for B

4.2 Preparing data for AI and ML.what_the_exam_tests: This section tests how you choose the lowest-operational-overhead ML path, prepare training data correctly, and make features usable for repeatable training and serving. Questions usually describe a workload constraint such as SQL-only team, no labeled data, millisecond online prediction, private-document search, PII, time-series ordering, drift, or skew, then ask which GCP service or preparation pattern is most appropriate. The highest-yield decision axes are pretrained vs BigQuery ML vs AutoML vs Vertex AI custom training, batch vs online features, BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH vs Vertex AI Vector Search, and evaluation/splitting choices that avoid leakage.
key_services: Vertex AI / Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform
Pretrained APIs (Vision, NLP, Speech, Translation, Document AI)
must_know_services: BigQuery - Primary warehouse for ML-ready data, SQL feature engineering, governed datasets, and BigQuery ML training where data already lives.
BigQuery ML - SQL-based model training, evaluation, prediction, forecasting, embeddings, remote models, and explainability without moving data out of BigQuery.
Vertex AI - Managed ML platform for custom training, AutoML, model registry, endpoints, batch prediction, pipelines, experiments, monitoring, and feature workflows.
Vertex AI custom training - Use when model architecture, framework, accelerators, distributed training, or custom containers exceed BigQuery ML and AutoML.
Vertex AI Feature Store - Central feature management for reusable online and offline features, point-in-time correctness, and reduced training-serving skew.
Vertex AI Vector Search - Low-latency approximate nearest neighbor search for large embedding indexes, recommendations, semantic search, and RAG retrieval.
BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH - SQL-native vector similarity search when embeddings and corpus data already live in BigQuery and interactive SQL latency is acceptable.
ML.GENERATE_EMBEDDING - BigQuery ML function for generating embeddings with remote Vertex AI models from SQL pipelines.
Document AI - Pretrained and custom document parsers for forms, invoices, receipts, contracts, OCR, and structured field extraction.
Cloud Vision API - Pretrained image labeling, OCR, object/localization, and safe-search tasks when custom image training is unnecessary.
Speech-to-Text - Pretrained audio transcription, streaming transcription, diarization, and call-center preparation for downstream analytics or ML.
Cloud Translation API - Pretrained translation and language detection for multilingual data preparation without building a model.
Cloud Natural Language API - Pretrained entity, sentiment, syntax, and classification analysis for text enrichment when the labels are standard.
Dataflow - Managed Apache Beam for scalable batch and streaming preprocessing, feature generation, windowing, joins, and Pub/Sub-to-BigQuery pipelines.
Dataproc - Managed Spark/Hadoop for existing Spark preprocessing jobs, MLlib workflows, or lift-and-shift pipelines with cluster control.
Pub/Sub - Event ingestion for streaming feature generation and near-real-time prediction pipelines.
Cloud Data Fusion - Low-code ETL/ELT pipeline authoring for data preparation when visual transformations and connectors matter more than custom code.
Sensitive Data Protection - Discovery, classification, masking, tokenization, and de-identification of PII before training or analytics.

read_content.heading: Simplest Capable ML Path
read_content.body:
Concept: The exam rewards the simplest service that satisfies the stated accuracy, customization, latency, governance, and operations constraints. The usual ladder is pretrained APIs first, then BigQuery ML when data is in BigQuery and SQL is enough, then AutoML when labels exist but the team does not want to manage model code, then Vertex AI custom training when the model itself must be controlled.
Use pretrained APIs when the task is a standard modality: translate text, transcribe audio, detect image labels, OCR documents, extract invoice fields, classify sentiment, or identify entities. Use BigQuery ML when a tabular dataset already sits in BigQuery and the team wants SQL training, SQL prediction, or in-warehouse forecasting. Use Vertex AI custom training when the question mentions PyTorch, TensorFlow custom layers, GPUs, TPUs, distributed training, custom containers, or a novel architecture.
Trade-offs: BigQuery ML minimizes movement and ops but is bounded by supported model types and SQL workflows. AutoML reduces model-code burden but still requires labeled examples and may be less transparent than hand-built models. Custom training gives full control but adds packaging, tuning, deployment, monitoring, and cost responsibility.
Exam traps: Do not choose custom training for invoice extraction, translation, OCR, basic image labeling, or speech transcription unless custom labels or accuracy requirements explicitly exceed pretrained capability. Do not move terabytes out of BigQuery to train in notebooks when BigQuery ML satisfies the model type.

Do not move terabytes out of BigQuery to train in notebooks when BigQuery ML satisfies the model type.

read_content.heading: Training Data Shape and Splits
read_content.body:
Concept: Preparing data for ML is mainly about creating a representative, leak-free, stable dataset. The exam expects you to know the difference between training, validation/evaluation, and test sets, and when random splitting is wrong. A common split pattern is 70/15/15 or 80/10/10, but the exact percentage matters less than isolation and representativeness.
Use random splits for independent, identically distributed tabular examples where each row is independent and no entity or time dependency leaks across splits. Use time-based splits for forecasting, churn over time, fraud over time, demand planning, clickstream modeling, and any case where future information cannot be available at prediction time. Use grouped splits when multiple rows belong to the same customer, device, patient, account, or transaction chain.
Trade-offs: Random splits maximize statistical mixing but can leak future behavior into training. Time-based splits better simulate production but can expose seasonality or concept drift that lowers metrics. Grouped splits avoid memorizing entity-specific signals but may reduce training volume.
Exam traps: If the question says time series, forecast, demand, seasonality, weekly, daily, or historical trend, do not choose random shuffle. If it says a model performs well offline but poorly after launch, suspect leakage, training-serving skew, non-representative training data, or concept drift before blaming compute size.

read_content.heading: Feature Engineering in BigQuery
read_content.body:
Concept: BigQuery is the default preparation layer when raw and curated data already live in the warehouse. The exam expects SQL transformations, joins, aggregations, window functions, materialized views, scheduled queries, and BigQuery ML TRANSFORM clauses to be favored over exporting data to custom systems. Feature engineering should be reproducible and governed, not a one-off notebook transformation.
Use BigQuery for ad hoc SQL exploration, large joins, feature backfills, cohort calculations, aggregations over partitioned tables, and SQL-native preprocessing before BigQuery ML. Partition large training tables by ingestion date or event date, cluster by high-filter columns such as customer_id, region, or model segment, and avoid SELECT * over wide raw tables when only a subset feeds training.
Trade-offs: BigQuery is excellent for batch feature creation and analysis, but not for sub-millisecond online feature lookup. Scheduled queries and materialized views simplify batch reuse, while Dataflow or Feature Store is better for streaming or online consistency. BigQuery ML TRANSFORM is valuable because the same preprocessing can be applied at training and prediction time.
Exam traps: If the question says SQL analysts, data already in BigQuery, no data movement, or train where data lives, choose BigQuery ML and SQL transformations. If it says the same feature must be served online for real-time prediction, BigQuery alone is usually insufficient; use Vertex AI Feature Store or an online serving pattern.

read_content.heading: BigQuery ML Core Model Choices
read_content.body:
Concept: BigQuery ML appears heavily because it lets data engineers train and run models using SQL. Know the model families and the clues: LINEAR_REG for numeric regression, LOGISTIC_REG for binary or multiclass classification, KMEANS for clustering, ARIMA_PLUS for time-series forecasting, MATRIX_FACTORIZATION for recommendations, boosted tree and random forest for stronger tabular supervised learning, DNN for neural tabular models, and remote models for calling Vertex AI or foundation models.
Use ML.EVALUATE for metrics, ML.PREDICT for inference, ML.FORECAST for ARIMA_PLUS forecasts, ML.EXPLAIN_PREDICT for feature attribution on supported models, and ML.TRANSFORM to persist transformations with the model. Use CREATE MODEL in SQL when the preparation, training, and batch prediction all fit a warehouse workflow.
Trade-offs: BigQuery ML is low ops and governed by BigQuery IAM, jobs, reservations, and audit logs. It is not the answer when the question requires custom training loops, unsupported architectures, custom loss functions, advanced computer vision, reinforcement learning, or specialized accelerator control.
Exam traps: Forecasting from a BigQuery table maps to ARIMA_PLUS, not a generic custom TensorFlow job. Unlabeled segmentation maps to KMEANS, not logistic regression. Recommendations from user-item interactions often map to MATRIX_FACTORIZATION, not k-means.

read_content.heading: Pretrained APIs and Document AI
read_content.body:
Concept: Pretrained APIs prepare unstructured data for analytics and ML without training a model. Document AI extracts text, layout, tables, entities, and structured fields from documents. Cloud Vision API handles image labels and OCR-style tasks. Speech-to-Text transcribes audio. Cloud Translation API translates text. Cloud Natural Language API enriches text with entity, sentiment, syntax, and classification signals.
Use these services when the business task is common and the prompt emphasizes speed, no ML expertise, no labeled training data, or minimal operations. Document AI is the stronger answer for invoices, receipts, forms, contracts, W-2s, statements, and document layout because it is built around processors and structured extraction. Vision API is the broader image API when the input is an image and the required output is labels, objects, or OCR text rather than document-specific fields.
Trade-offs: Pretrained APIs are fast and managed but less customizable. Custom processors or custom models may be needed when domain-specific fields, unusual document formats, or custom labels must be learned. Outputs often need validation, confidence thresholds, human review, and downstream normalization before they become training labels or features.
Exam traps: Do not build an OCR model in Vertex AI custom training when the question only asks to extract text or fields from invoices. Do not choose Cloud Natural Language API for document table extraction; choose Document AI. Do not choose Translation API when the requirement is semantic search; translation is preprocessing, not retrieval.

read_content.heading: AutoML and Custom Training
read_content.body:
Concept: AutoML and Vertex AI custom training sit above BigQuery ML in flexibility and operational burden. AutoML is for teams with labeled data that want Google-managed model search and deployment for supported modalities. Custom training is for teams that own the code, framework, architecture, dependencies, and training loop.
Use AutoML when the question says custom labels, little ML expertise, managed training, good baseline, tabular/image/video/text classification, or object detection without hand-written model code. Use Vertex AI custom training when the prompt says custom PyTorch/TensorFlow model, custom container, custom preprocessing code, distributed training, GPUs/TPUs, hyperparameter tuning, experiment tracking, or a model type not available in BigQuery ML or AutoML.
Trade-offs: AutoML can reduce engineering effort but needs enough labeled data and may not expose all internals. Custom training supports accelerators and exact modeling choices but requires containerization, data access design, artifact management, reproducibility, and cost control. Vertex AI Training jobs can run managed custom containers or prebuilt containers; outputs should be registered in Vertex AI Model Registry.
Exam traps: If the question says the team only knows SQL and the data is in BigQuery, do not jump to AutoML or custom training unless required model types exceed BigQuery ML. If it says custom neural architecture or nonstandard loss function, AutoML is too constrained.

read_content.heading: Feature Store and Training-Serving Skew
read_content.body:
Concept: Training-serving skew happens when features used in training differ from features computed during prediction. The exam frequently disguises it as a model with high offline accuracy but poor online behavior, duplicate feature logic in Spark and Python, inconsistent joins, time zone differences, late-arriving events, or different aggregation windows. Vertex AI Feature Store is the canonical managed answer for reusable online and offline features.
Use Vertex AI Feature Store when multiple models or teams need shared features, when online predictions need low-latency feature lookups, or when training data must be generated from historical point-in-time values. Offline serving supports training datasets with historical correctness; online serving supports fresh lookups for real-time prediction. Point-in-time correctness prevents using a feature value that was updated after the prediction timestamp.
Trade-offs: Feature Store adds modeling and governance around entities, features, and feature views, which is worthwhile for repeated production ML but may be excessive for a one-off batch model fully contained in BigQuery. BigQuery tables can be fine as an offline feature store, but they do not automatically solve online serving or skew.
Exam traps: If features are recomputed separately for training and serving, choose Feature Store or a single shared transformation path. Model Monitoring detects skew and drift; Feature Store helps prevent skew by standardizing feature definitions and serving.

read_content.heading: Streaming Preparation for ML
read_content.body:
Concept: Streaming ML preparation is about ingesting events, applying windowed transformations, and writing low-latency features or predictions. Pub/Sub usually ingests events, Dataflow processes them with Apache Beam, BigQuery stores analytical results, and Vertex AI Endpoints or Feature Store serve online prediction workflows.
Use Pub/Sub plus Dataflow when the exam says unbounded events, real-time enrichment, session windows, sliding windows, event-time processing, late data, exactly-once-style pipeline semantics, autoscaling stream processing, or feature updates within seconds. Use BigQuery streaming or Storage Write API when events should land quickly in warehouse tables for analytics and batch ML. Use Cloud Run or Cloud Functions only for lightweight event handlers, not stateful large-scale windowed feature computation.
Trade-offs: Dataflow is more operationally managed and robust for streaming transforms than self-managed Spark, but it requires Beam pipeline design. Dataproc Streaming or Spark Structured Streaming may be reasonable when the organization already has Spark code and needs compatibility. BigQuery is excellent for near-real-time analytics but not a substitute for stateful streaming feature engineering when strict event-time logic is required.
Exam traps: If the question says sessionization, late events, watermarks, or windowed aggregates, Dataflow is usually the answer. If it says sub-1-second p99 serving feature lookup, do not rely on ad hoc BigQuery queries.

read_content.heading: Embeddings and RAG Preparation
read_content.body:
Concept: Retrieval-augmented generation preparation converts private data into searchable chunks, embeds those chunks, retrieves the most relevant matches, and grounds a model response with retrieved context. The pipeline is usually parse documents, chunk text, generate embeddings, store vectors with metadata, retrieve top-k chunks, filter by permissions, and pass grounded context to Gemini or another model.
Use ML.GENERATE_EMBEDDING when the corpus is in BigQuery and the team wants SQL-native embedding generation. Use BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH when the data, vectors, metadata filters, and evaluation can stay in BigQuery and second-level analytical latency is acceptable. Use Vertex AI Vector Search for large-scale approximate nearest neighbor search, low-latency retrieval, high QPS, and production serving.
Trade-offs: BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH is simpler for warehouse-native semantic analytics and governance. Vertex AI Vector Search is more serving-oriented and built for large indexes and millisecond-style retrieval, but adds index lifecycle management and integration work. RAG does not fine-tune the model; it retrieves external context at request time.
Exam traps: If the question says chatbot over private documents, do not answer only with fine-tuning. If it says answers must reflect frequently changing documents, RAG is stronger than tuning because re-indexing/retrieval updates knowledge without retraining. Always include metadata filtering for access control when documents have per-user permissions.

read_content.heading: Vector Index and Similarity Trade-offs
read_content.body:
Concept: Vector search preparation is not just storing embeddings. You need consistent embedding models, normalized chunks, metadata for filters, refresh strategy, distance metric alignment, and evaluation of retrieval quality. A vector from one embedding model is not comparable to a vector from another model unless the index is rebuilt or separated.
Use Vertex AI Vector Search when the prompt says billions of vectors, high QPS, low-latency semantic search, recommendation retrieval, approximate nearest neighbor, or production RAG serving. Use BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH when analysts need SQL joins between vectors and warehouse facts, governance through BigQuery, batch similarity jobs, or quick semantic search over tables. Use brute-force exact search only for small datasets or evaluation baselines.
Trade-offs: Approximate nearest neighbor search improves latency and scale but may trade exact recall for speed. Exact search gives precise nearest neighbors but becomes expensive at high cardinality. Chunk size affects retrieval: chunks too small lose context; chunks too large dilute relevance and increase prompt cost. Metadata filters improve security and relevance but can reduce recall if too restrictive.
Exam traps: Do not choose Cloud Search for embedding similarity unless the question is about enterprise search integration rather than ML vector retrieval. Do not store embeddings without a plan for re-embedding when the model or chunking strategy changes.
read_content.body_html:
Concept: Vector search preparation is not just storing embeddings. You need consistent embedding models, normalized chunks, metadata for filters, refresh strategy, distance metric alignment, and evaluation of retrieval quality. A vector from one embedding model is not comparable to a vector from another model unless the index is rebuilt or separated.

Use Vertex AI Vector Search when the prompt says billions of vectors, high QPS, low-latency semantic search, recommendation retrieval, approximate nearest neighbor, or production RAG serving. Use BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH when analysts need SQL joins between vectors and warehouse facts, governance through BigQuery, batch similarity jobs, or quick semantic search over tables. Use brute-force exact search only for small datasets or evaluation baselines.

Trade-offs: Approximate nearest neighbor search improves latency and scale but may trade exact recall for speed. Exact search gives precise nearest neighbors but becomes expensive at high cardinality. Chunk size affects retrieval: chunks too small lose context; chunks too large dilute relevance and increase prompt cost. Metadata filters improve security and relevance but can reduce recall if too restrictive.

Exam traps: Do not choose Cloud Search for embedding similarity unless the question is about enterprise search integration rather than ML vector retrieval. Do not store embeddings without a plan for re-embedding when the model or chunking strategy changes.

read_content.heading: Data Quality and Label Quality
read_content.body:
Concept: ML preparation requires data quality checks before model training. The exam may describe missing values, inconsistent categories, duplicates, outliers, class imbalance, noisy labels, schema drift, or training data not matching serving traffic. The right answer is usually to validate and correct the data pipeline, not increase machine size or train longer.
Use BigQuery profiling queries, Dataplex data quality, Dataform assertions, Cloud Composer orchestration, or pipeline validation steps to detect null spikes, invalid ranges, duplicate keys, referential failures, and schema changes. Use label review, active learning, confusion matrices, and human-in-the-loop workflows when labels are inconsistent or subjective. Use class weights, resampling, threshold tuning, or better metrics when classes are imbalanced.
Trade-offs: Cleaning aggressively can remove rare but important examples, especially fraud or anomaly data. Imputation preserves row count but can encode bias if missingness is informative. Oversampling can improve minority recall but may overfit; undersampling can lose information. Accuracy is often the wrong metric for imbalanced classification; precision, recall, F1, ROC AUC, PR AUC, and business cost are stronger.
Exam traps: More epochs do not fix bad labels, leakage, imbalance, or train-serving skew. If the prompt says 99% accuracy but misses rare fraud, the giveaway is class imbalance; choose precision/recall-oriented evaluation and resampling/class weighting.

read_content.heading: PII and Sensitive Data for ML
read_content.body:
Concept: Preparing data for AI/ML must protect sensitive data before it becomes training input, prompt context, embeddings, logs, or exported datasets. The exam distinguishes governance controls from actual de-identification. IAM, column-level security, row-level security, and policy tags restrict access; Sensitive Data Protection transforms or masks sensitive values.
Use Sensitive Data Protection to inspect, classify, mask, tokenize, pseudonymize, or de-identify PII such as names, emails, phone numbers, SSNs, credit cards, and health identifiers. Use BigQuery policy tags for column-level access control, row access policies for row filtering, authorized views for limited projections, and CMEK when encryption key control is required. Use data minimization: train on only the fields needed, remove direct identifiers, and avoid putting sensitive raw text into embeddings unless the retrieval layer enforces access.
Trade-offs: Hashing can preserve joins but may be vulnerable for small domains unless salted or tokenized. Tokenization preserves reversibility under controls; masking is safer for broad analytics but can reduce feature value. Embeddings can leak semantic content, so access controls must cover vector stores and source documents.
Exam traps: If the question says protect PII before ML training, column-level ACL alone is not enough if the model still trains on the raw value. If it says analysts should not see PII but pipelines may process it, combine IAM/policy tags with de-identification where needed.

read_content.heading: Batch vs Online Prediction Preparation
read_content.body:
Concept: The exam often asks whether to prepare data for batch prediction or online prediction. Batch prediction scores many rows asynchronously, usually from BigQuery or Cloud Storage, and writes results back for analysis or downstream use. Online prediction serves one request or a small batch synchronously behind a low-latency endpoint.
Use batch prediction when the prompt says nightly scoring, weekly churn list, daily demand forecast, millions of customers, no immediate user-facing response, or write predictions to BigQuery. Use Vertex AI Endpoints when the prompt says real-time recommendation, fraud decision at checkout, personalization during a session, low-latency API, canary deployment, traffic splitting, or autoscaling serving. Use BigQuery ML ML.PREDICT when prediction can run inside SQL as part of analytics or batch pipelines.
Trade-offs: Batch prediction is cheaper and simpler for large offline jobs, but it cannot react to per-request context unless features are precomputed frequently. Online prediction supports fresh request features but requires endpoint deployment, feature serving, monitoring, and latency management. Vertex AI Endpoints can support traffic splitting across model versions for gradual rollout.
Exam traps: If the question says p99 latency under 100 ms or user-facing response, avoid BigQuery ad hoc prediction. If it says score all customers overnight, an always-on endpoint is unnecessary unless the same model also serves online traffic.

read_content.heading: Pipelines, Registry, and Reproducibility
read_content.body:
Concept: Preparing data for production AI/ML includes repeatable workflows, not just a trained model. Vertex AI Pipelines orchestrates preprocessing, training, evaluation, validation, and deployment steps. Vertex AI Model Registry tracks model versions, metadata, aliases, and deployment targets. Vertex AI Experiments tracks training runs, parameters, and metrics.
Use Vertex AI Pipelines when the exam says repeatable training, CI/CD for ML, scheduled retraining, approval gates, lineage, or reproducible DAG. Use Model Registry when it says version models, compare versions, roll back, deploy approved model, or govern lifecycle. Use Cloud Composer when the workflow spans many non-ML systems and Airflow is already the orchestration standard; use Vertex AI Pipelines for ML-native pipeline metadata and integration.
Trade-offs: A notebook is acceptable for exploration but weak for production reproducibility. Cloud Build can build containers and trigger pipeline runs, but it is not the ML pipeline system itself. Model Registry does not monitor drift; it stores and organizes model artifacts and versions.
Exam traps: If the question asks how to automate retraining after new data arrives, pick a pipeline with validation and registry steps, not a manual notebook. If it asks how to compare experiments, choose Vertex AI Experiments rather than Cloud Logging.

read_content.heading: Monitoring Drift, Skew, and Decay
read_content.body:
Concept: Production model preparation includes baselines and monitoring plans. Vertex AI Model Monitoring detects training-serving skew, prediction drift, and feature distribution changes for deployed models. It can compare live prediction inputs to training data or a recent baseline and send alerts through Cloud Monitoring.
Use Model Monitoring when the prompt says detect model decay, feature drift after launch, input distribution changed, production predictions differ from training expectations, or alert when prediction data no longer resembles training data. Use ML.EVALUATE, evaluation tables, and scheduled BigQuery checks for BigQuery ML models and batch scoring workflows. Use Cloud Monitoring for service metrics such as latency, error rate, and endpoint resource use, but that does not replace statistical model monitoring.
Trade-offs: Monitoring detects issues after deployment; it does not fix bad feature definitions or leakage. Feature Store and shared transformations reduce skew risk before deployment. Retraining can help concept drift, but only after confirming data quality, labels, and business process changes.
Exam traps: If the phrase is training-serving skew, the answer is not just more training data. If it asks for model version rollback, use Model Registry and endpoint traffic controls; if it asks for distribution detection, use Model Monitoring.

read_content.heading: Evaluation Metrics and Explainability
read_content.body:
Concept: Preparing for AI/ML includes selecting metrics that match the business problem. Regression uses RMSE, MAE, MSE, or R2. Classification uses accuracy, precision, recall, F1, ROC AUC, PR AUC, log loss, and confusion matrices. Forecasting uses metrics such as MAE, RMSE, MAPE, and forecast horizon comparisons. Ranking/recommendation may use precision@k, recall@k, NDCG, or hit rate.
Use precision when false positives are expensive, such as blocking legitimate transactions. Use recall when false negatives are expensive, such as missed fraud or disease detection. Use PR AUC instead of ROC AUC when the positive class is rare and the exam emphasizes imbalanced data. Use explainability when the question says stakeholders need feature influence, regulatory explanation, debugging, or trust.
Trade-offs: A single metric can hide unacceptable behavior in subgroups or rare classes. Threshold tuning changes precision/recall without retraining. Explainability can identify leakage or unstable features, but not all model types support identical explanation methods. In BigQuery ML, ML.EXPLAIN_PREDICT is the clue for feature attribution on supported models.
Exam traps: If the model has high accuracy on a 99:1 dataset, accuracy is a distractor. If the question says explain why individual predictions happen in BigQuery ML, choose ML.EXPLAIN_PREDICT rather than exporting to a custom explainability service.

read_content.heading: Data Movement and Storage Format
read_content.body:
Concept: The PDE exam frequently favors keeping data where it already lives, especially for BigQuery-based ML. Moving data between systems increases cost, latency, governance complexity, and pipeline failure modes. Preparation should use managed connectors, columnar formats, and partitioning that match the training and serving pattern.
Use BigQuery tables for warehouse-native training, SQL features, and batch prediction. Use Cloud Storage with Parquet, Avro, TFRecord, CSV, or JSON when custom training jobs need files, when data is shared across tools, or when unstructured objects must be staged. Use Dataproc or Dataflow for large preprocessing before writing curated data to BigQuery or Cloud Storage. Use BigQuery Storage API or managed Vertex AI integrations instead of hand-rolled exports when possible.
Trade-offs: CSV is portable but loses schema richness and is inefficient for large analytical reads. Parquet and Avro preserve schema and compress well; Parquet is common for columnar analytics. TFRecord is common for TensorFlow at scale. Exporting BigQuery data to Cloud Storage may be appropriate for custom training but unnecessary for BigQuery ML.
Exam traps: If the question says minimize data movement and the model is supported by BigQuery ML, do not export to Cloud Storage for Vertex AI training. If it says existing Spark preprocessing must be reused, Dataproc may beat rewriting everything in Dataflow.

read_content.heading: Governed AI Preparation Patterns
read_content.body:
Concept: ML data preparation must be governed across datasets, features, models, and predictions. The exam often combines AI preparation with IAM, lineage, auditability, retention, and least privilege. The right answer usually uses managed GCP controls rather than ad hoc scripts or shared service accounts.
Use Dataplex for data governance, cataloging, quality, and lake/warehouse organization. Use Data Catalog-style metadata and policy tags through BigQuery governance flows. Use IAM at dataset, table, column, model, endpoint, and service account boundaries. Use audit logs to track access and administrative actions. Use CMEK if the question explicitly requires customer-managed encryption keys. Use VPC Service Controls when exfiltration risk across service perimeters is central.
Trade-offs: Governance controls do not replace data preparation. A policy tag can hide a column from analysts, but the ML pipeline may still need de-identified values. CMEK controls key ownership but does not anonymize data. VPC Service Controls reduce exfiltration risk but do not solve row-level access or model bias.
Exam traps: If the question says comply with privacy and still train, the answer is usually de-identification plus access controls, not only encryption. If it says audit who accessed training data, use Cloud Audit Logs and BigQuery audit metadata rather than custom application logs.

choose_this_when: Pretrained APIs - Standard task such as OCR, translation, speech transcription, entity/sentiment extraction, image labels, or invoice fields; no custom model required.
Document AI - The input is a document with layout, tables, forms, receipts, invoices, contracts, or structured fields to extract.
BigQuery ML - Data is already in BigQuery, the team uses SQL, the model type is supported, and the requirement is low-ops training or batch prediction.
BigQuery ML ARIMA_PLUS - Forecasting demand, volume, revenue, usage, or inventory from time-indexed BigQuery tables.
BigQuery ML KMEANS - Unlabeled segmentation, clustering, anomaly-style grouping, or exploratory customer/device grouping in SQL.
Vertex AI AutoML - Labeled examples exist, the team wants custom labels, and they prefer managed training over writing model code.
Vertex AI custom training - The prompt mentions custom architecture, custom loss, PyTorch/TensorFlow code, custom containers, GPUs/TPUs, distributed training, or unsupported model types.
Vertex AI Feature Store - Features must be reused across teams/models, served online at low latency, or generated point-in-time correctly for training.
Dataflow - Streaming preprocessing, event-time windows, sessionization, late events, Pub/Sub ingestion, and scalable batch/stream transformations.
Dataproc - Existing Spark/Hadoop code, MLlib pipelines, cluster-level dependency control, or lift-and-shift preprocessing.
BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH - Embeddings and metadata are in BigQuery, SQL retrieval is needed, and latency can be analytical rather than strict online serving.
Vertex AI Vector Search - Large-scale ANN retrieval, high QPS, low latency, recommendation retrieval, semantic search, or production RAG.
ML.GENERATE_EMBEDDING - Generate embeddings directly in BigQuery SQL using Vertex AI remote models.
Vertex AI Model Monitoring - Detect training-serving skew, prediction drift, feature drift, or production distribution changes on deployed models.
Sensitive Data Protection - Discover, mask, tokenize, or de-identify PII before analytics, training, embeddings, or prompt construction.
Vertex AI Pipelines - Repeatable preprocessing/training/evaluation/deployment DAGs, scheduled retraining, lineage, and approval gates.
avoid_this_when: Avoid Vertex AI custom training when Document AI, Vision API, Translation API, Speech-to-Text, or Natural Language API solves the standard task with no training data.
Avoid random train/test splits for forecasting, time-series, session histories, or future-dependent labels; use chronological or grouped splits.
Avoid BigQuery ad hoc prediction for user-facing p99 latency under 100 ms; use Vertex AI Endpoints and online features.
Avoid BigQuery-only feature lookup when the scenario requires millisecond online serving or consistent online/offline features across models.
Avoid AutoML when the requirement is a custom architecture, custom loss function, custom training loop, or unsupported framework behavior.
Avoid Cloud Run or Cloud Functions for large stateful streaming feature computation with windows and late data; use Dataflow.
Avoid column-level security as the only PII protection when raw sensitive values still train the model or enter embeddings.
Avoid tuning or more epochs as the answer to class imbalance, label noise, leakage, drift, or skew.
Avoid fine-tuning as the primary answer for a chatbot over changing private documents; use RAG with embeddings and retrieval.
Avoid exporting terabytes from BigQuery to custom training when BigQuery ML supports the model and SQL workflow.
Avoid using accuracy alone for rare-event classification; use precision, recall, F1, PR AUC, and threshold tuning.
Avoid serving a model from an unmanaged VM when Vertex AI Endpoints provides managed scaling, versioning, traffic splitting, and monitoring.
common_gotchas: Don't build a custom model when DocAI/Vision/Translation handles it.
Train/test split on time-series must respect time order.
Class imbalance: use class weights or resampling, not just more epochs.
common_traps: Custom training for invoice extraction; giveaway phrase: invoices, receipts, forms, structured fields, OCR. Use Document AI.
Random split for time-series; giveaway phrase: forecast, demand, seasonality, next week, historical trend. Use chronological split and ARIMA_PLUS when in BigQuery.
Accuracy as the metric for rare positives; giveaway phrase: 99% accuracy but misses fraud. Use recall, precision, F1, PR AUC, class weighting, or resampling.
BigQuery for sub-100 ms online prediction; giveaway phrase: user-facing real-time decision or p99 latency. Use Vertex AI Endpoints and online features.
Policy tags as de-identification; giveaway phrase: PII must not be used in training. Use Sensitive Data Protection transformations, not only access control.
More epochs for data quality problems; giveaway phrase: noisy labels, missing values, leakage, skew, class imbalance. Fix data and metrics first.
Fine-tuning for changing private documents; giveaway phrase: internal docs update frequently. Use RAG and re-indexing instead of retraining for knowledge updates.
Cloud Functions for stateful streaming windows; giveaway phrase: sessions, late events, watermarks, sliding windows. Use Dataflow.
Model Registry for drift detection; giveaway phrase: detect feature distribution changes. Use Vertex AI Model Monitoring.
Model Monitoring to prevent skew; giveaway phrase: separate feature code paths. Use Feature Store/shared transformations to prevent, monitoring to detect.
Cloud Vision API for invoice table extraction; giveaway phrase: line items, fields, forms, layout. Use Document AI.
AutoML for custom loss or architecture; giveaway phrase: custom TensorFlow/PyTorch layer, nonstandard loss. Use Vertex AI custom training.
Exporting BigQuery data for supported SQL ML; giveaway phrase: minimize data movement, SQL analysts. Use BigQuery ML.
Unsecured vector store for private docs; giveaway phrase: different users have different document access. Use metadata filters and IAM-aware retrieval.

parse receipts, forms, or contracts

sentiment or entities from text
train where the data lives in BigQuery

recommend products from user-item interactions
custom PyTorch model with GPUs
little ML expertise but custom labels
reuse features across multiple models
offline features differ from online features
detect feature drift after deployment
chat over private documents
clue_words.answer: RAG with embeddings and vector search

clue_words.phrase: SQL semantic search over warehouse data
billions of embeddings and low latency
streaming session windows from events
mask or tokenize PII before training

Need recommendations from user-item interaction matrix in BigQuery
Need custom labels but no model-code management

Need to detect training-serving skew or feature drift after deployment

service_decision_table.answer: Batch prediction or BigQuery ML ML.PREDICT

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need low-latency user-facing prediction API
Need streaming windows, sessions, and late-event handling
Need reuse existing Spark preprocessing
Need SQL-native embeddings over BigQuery text

scenario_patterns.how_question_disguises_it: A business team needs text translated, audio transcribed, images labeled, or invoice fields extracted quickly with no ML staff. The distractor is Vertex AI custom training; the correct move is the relevant pretrained API, usually Document AI for documents.

All curated data is already in BigQuery, analysts know SQL, and the model is regression, classification, clustering, recommendation, or forecasting. The disguised test is avoiding unnecessary export to Cloud Storage or custom training.

The prompt mentions demand, seasonality, daily sales, inventory, or future time periods and offers random split as an option. The right reasoning is chronological split and BigQuery ML ARIMA_PLUS when data is in BigQuery.

Offline metrics are strong, but online predictions degrade because batch Spark features and serving Python features differ. The answer is shared feature definitions with Vertex AI Feature Store and monitoring with Vertex AI Model Monitoring.

Events arrive continuously from apps or devices and the model needs rolling counts, sessions, or windowed aggregates. Pub/Sub plus Dataflow is the preparation path; BigQuery alone is not the windowed streaming engine.

Users ask natural-language questions over internal documents that change frequently and have access restrictions. The pattern is RAG: parse, chunk, embed, store vectors with metadata, retrieve authorized chunks, and ground Gemini.

The scenario asks for similar items, nearest neighbors, or recommendations over millions to billions of embeddings at high QPS. The correct service is Vertex AI Vector Search rather than exact SQL scans.

Analysts want semantic search or similarity joins over text already stored in BigQuery and can tolerate query-job latency. The answer is ML.GENERATE_EMBEDDING plus BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH, not a separate serving index.

A dataset contains emails, phone numbers, SSNs, or health identifiers and must be used for ML while reducing exposure. The answer combines Sensitive Data Protection de-identification with BigQuery governance; encryption or IAM alone is incomplete.

The model reports very high accuracy but misses fraud, failures, abuse, or rare positives. The correct response is class imbalance handling and precision/recall or PR AUC evaluation, not bigger machines or more epochs.

exam_pattern_notes: Mentions data already in BigQuery plus SQL team plus supported model type -> BigQuery ML, not Vertex AI custom training.
Mentions standard document extraction -> Document AI, not a custom OCR model and not generic Vision API unless fields/layout are irrelevant.
Mentions custom architecture, custom loss, custom container, or accelerator control -> Vertex AI custom training, not AutoML.
Mentions labeled data but limited ML expertise -> Vertex AI AutoML unless BigQuery ML is enough and data is already in BigQuery.
Mentions forecasting with timestamps -> preserve time order and use ARIMA_PLUS for BigQuery-native forecasting.
Mentions future data accidentally used during training -> data leakage; fix split/features, not compute size.
Mentions offline accuracy high and production accuracy low -> investigate training-serving skew, drift, leakage, and representative data.
Mentions same features for training and serving -> Vertex AI Feature Store or shared transformation path.
Mentions drift or skew detection after deployment -> Vertex AI Model Monitoring with Cloud Monitoring alerts.
Mentions real-time p99 latency under 100 ms -> Vertex AI Endpoint plus online features; avoid ad hoc BigQuery scoring.
Mentions nightly scoring or weekly customer list -> batch prediction or BigQuery ML ML.PREDICT; endpoint may be unnecessary.
Mentions private docs or knowledge that changes frequently -> RAG with embeddings and vector retrieval, not fine-tuning as the main answer.
Mentions BigQuery corpus and SQL similarity search -> ML.GENERATE_EMBEDDING plus BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH.
Mentions billions of embeddings or high QPS retrieval -> Vertex AI Vector Search, not exact SQL vector scan.
final_review_bullets: Simplest capable ladder: pretrained API -> BigQuery ML or AutoML -> Vertex AI custom training.
Data already in BigQuery plus SQL team means BigQuery ML unless custom model requirements exceed it.
ARIMA_PLUS is the BigQuery ML forecasting clue.
KMEANS is the unlabeled clustering/segmentation clue.
MATRIX_FACTORIZATION is the BigQuery ML recommendation clue for user-item interactions.
Document AI beats Vision API for invoices, receipts, forms, tables, and structured document fields.
Feature Store prevents training-serving skew; Model Monitoring detects skew/drift after deployment.
Time-series split must preserve time order; random shuffle leaks future data.
Rare fraud with high accuracy means class imbalance; optimize precision/recall or PR AUC, not accuracy.
RAG equals chunk -> embed -> vector search -> retrieve -> ground Gemini response.
BigQuery VECTOR_SEARCH is SQL-native; Vertex AI Vector Search is low-latency production ANN retrieval.
ML.GENERATE_EMBEDDING generates embeddings from SQL in BigQuery.
Batch prediction scores many records asynchronously; online prediction uses Vertex AI Endpoints for low latency.
Dataflow is the streaming/windowing preparation answer for Pub/Sub events.
Sensitive Data Protection de-identifies PII; IAM and policy tags only control access.
Vertex AI Pipelines is production repeatability; notebooks are exploration, not governed retraining.

4.3 Sharing data.what_the_exam_tests: This section tests how to share BigQuery data and streaming data without unnecessary copies while preserving least privilege. Expect scenario questions that contrast authorized views, authorized datasets, Analytics Hub, row-access policies, policy tags, dynamic data masking, IAM, and export-based sharing. The decision axes are audience boundary, freshness, copy tolerance, row versus column restriction, producer control, and whether consumers need SQL, marketplace discovery, or event subscriptions.
must_know_services: BigQuery - Core analytical store where most sharing scenarios use dataset IAM, views, row-access policies, policy tags, and linked datasets.
Authorized views - BigQuery views that can read source tables without giving consumers direct access to the source dataset.
Authorized datasets - BigQuery dataset-level authorization that lets every authorized view in one dataset access protected tables in another dataset.
Analytics Hub - BigQuery data exchange for zero-copy dataset sharing and Pub/Sub topic sharing through listings and subscriptions.
Linked datasets - Subscriber-side BigQuery datasets created from Analytics Hub listings; read-only pointers to publisher-managed data.
Pub/Sub - Messaging service for event streams; can be published through Analytics Hub when the exam says streaming data sharing.
Row-access policies - BigQuery table policies that filter rows per grantee using SQL predicates.
Policy tags - Data Catalog taxonomy tags used by BigQuery for column-level access control and governance classification.
Dynamic data masking - BigQuery masking rules that return masked values for protected columns instead of exposing raw sensitive data.
Sensitive Data Protection - Discovery, inspection, de-identification, and tokenization service for PII before broad sharing or publishing.
IAM - Baseline access control for projects, datasets, tables, views, exchanges, listings, and subscriptions; choose narrow scopes.
Data Catalog - Metadata and taxonomy layer behind policy tags and data discovery/governance patterns.
Cloud Storage - Common but usually wrong sharing answer when the requirement says fresh SQL access or zero-copy; relevant for file exports.
Storage Transfer Service - Moves data between storage systems; not a governance or zero-copy sharing mechanism.
Dataplex Universal Catalog - Governance and discovery surface that may appear with enterprise data catalog and policy management questions.
VPC Service Controls - Perimeter control to reduce data exfiltration risk around BigQuery and Cloud Storage, not a row/column sharing feature.

read_content.heading: Sharing Decision Model
read_content.body:
Start every sharing question by identifying four facts: who consumes the data, what surface they need, what must be hidden, and whether copies are allowed. BigQuery sharing is usually solved without exporting files: dataset IAM for whole datasets, authorized views for curated SQL slices, authorized datasets for many curated views, row-access policies for per-principal row filters, policy tags for protected columns, and Analytics Hub for managed publishing to many consumers.

Use the narrowest primitive that satisfies the stated audience. Same-team access to all tables can be dataset-level IAM. A business unit that needs only aggregated columns should query an authorized view. A partner marketplace or many internal teams should subscribe through Analytics Hub. A multi-region or multi-tenant table that must stay single-copy should use row-access policies and policy tags.

Copy-based answers are usually distractors unless the requirement explicitly says offline delivery, file interchange, or data must be transformed into an external format. Exporting CSV to Cloud Storage creates drift, duplicates governance, and weakens auditability. Exam trap: if the prompt says "latest data," "single source of truth," "avoid ETL," "no duplicate storage," or "publisher retains control," prefer zero-copy sharing over exports, scheduled queries, or Dataflow copy pipelines.

read_content.heading: BigQuery IAM Scope
read_content.body:
BigQuery IAM is the baseline for sharing but rarely the complete answer for restricted sharing. Grant roles at the narrowest practical scope: dataset-level access when consumers may read the whole dataset, table-level access for a specific table, view access for a curated result, and project-level access only for administrative or broad internal roles. The exam often includes a tempting project-level bigquery.dataViewer grant; that is wrong when the consumer needs one dataset or a filtered slice.

Common roles matter: BigQuery Data Viewer can read table data and metadata at the granted scope; BigQuery Job User is commonly needed at the project level so a principal can run query jobs; BigQuery Data Owner or Admin is too broad for read-only sharing. Consumers of authorized views need access to the view and permission to run jobs, not direct access to the base table.

IAM vs row/column controls: IAM answers "can this principal access this object?" Row-access policies answer "which rows are visible?" Policy tags answer "which columns are visible or masked?" Analytics Hub answers "how do publishers distribute governed data products at scale?" Trap: IAM alone does not express "each region sees only its own rows" unless you create separate tables or datasets, which adds copy and operational burden.

Analytics Hub answers "how do publishers distribute governed data products at scale?" Trap: IAM alone does not express "each region sees only its own rows" unless you create separate tables or datasets, which adds copy and operational burden.

read_content.heading: Authorized Views
read_content.body:
An authorized view is a BigQuery view that is granted permission to read source tables in another dataset. Users query the view, and the view's SQL determines which rows, columns, joins, or aggregates are exposed. The consumer does not need direct permission on the base dataset. This is the classic answer for "share aggregate results but hide source rows" or "let analysts query a cleaned projection without seeing PII columns."

Use authorized views when the sharing contract is a stable SQL shape: selected columns, filtered rows, aggregated metrics, or a joined business-friendly surface. They are also useful when you want to centralize logic, such as masking with SQL expressions or excluding restricted columns. Consumers still need permission to the view dataset and permission to run query jobs.

Authorized view vs row-access policy: authorized views create a curated object that consumers intentionally query; row-access policies transparently filter the base table for different principals. Authorized view vs materialized copy: a view remains current and avoids duplicate storage, but complex view logic can add query cost and performance overhead because the query resolves against underlying data. Exam trap: granting the user access to both the view and the source dataset defeats the point if the requirement says source tables must remain hidden.

Users query the view, and the view's SQL determines which rows, columns, joins, or aggregates are exposed. The consumer does not need direct permission on the base dataset. This is the classic answer for "share aggregate results but hide source rows" or "let analysts query a cleaned projection without seeing PII columns."

They are also useful when you want to centralize logic, such as masking with SQL expressions or excluding restricted columns. Consumers still need permission to the view dataset and permission to run query jobs.

Authorized view vs row-access policy: authorized views create a curated object that consumers intentionally query; row-access policies transparently filter the base table for different principals. Authorized view vs materialized copy: a view remains current and avoids duplicate storage, but complex view logic can add query cost and performance overhead because the query resolves against underlying data. Exam trap: granting the user access to both the view and the source dataset defeats the point if the requirement says source tables must remain hidden.

read_content.heading: Authorized Datasets
read_content.body:
Authorized datasets extend the authorized-view pattern from one view to a whole dataset of views. Instead of authorizing each view individually against the source dataset, you authorize a dataset that contains multiple views. Every view in that authorized dataset can access the protected source tables according to its SQL definition. This reduces per-view maintenance when a team publishes many curated views over the same source.

Choose authorized datasets when the prompt says "many views," "several teams create shared views," "reduce operational overhead," or "avoid updating source dataset access for every new view." It is still a BigQuery view-based sharing pattern: consumers query the views, not the source tables. It works well for a governed semantic layer where a producer team owns source tables and a publishing team owns curated views.

Authorized dataset vs dataset IAM: dataset IAM grants access to data objects in that dataset; authorized datasets grant views in one dataset the right to read source data elsewhere. Authorized dataset vs Analytics Hub: authorized datasets help structure internal BigQuery permissions, while Analytics Hub packages and publishes data products to subscribers. Trap: do not use authorized datasets as a broad delegation mechanism without controlling who can create or modify views in the authorized dataset; a poorly governed view author could expose more than intended.

Trap: do not use authorized datasets as a broad delegation mechanism without controlling who can create or modify views in the authorized dataset; a poorly governed view author could expose more than intended.

read_content.heading: Analytics Hub Dataset Sharing
read_content.body:
Analytics Hub is the managed data exchange service for BigQuery sharing. Publishers create exchanges and listings; subscribers access listings as linked datasets. The linked dataset is a read-only pointer to the publisher's shared dataset, so data is not copied into the subscriber project. This is the answer when the question says "zero-copy," "many subscribers," "data marketplace," "partner sharing," "cross-organization," "central producer controls access," or "discoverable curated data products."

Use Analytics Hub when the audience is broader than a single hand-managed view permission. It supports private internal exchanges, organization-wide sharing, and controlled external sharing. Subscribers can query fresh publisher-managed data without maintaining ingestion pipelines. Publisher-side revocation and listing management are central to the design.

Analytics Hub vs authorized view: Analytics Hub distributes data products; authorized views restrict a SQL slice. They can be combined: publish a curated dataset or views through Analytics Hub when consumers should subscribe rather than receive direct dataset grants. Analytics Hub vs copying to Cloud Storage: Analytics Hub preserves freshness and governance, while exports create stale copies. Exam trap: if the prompt says subscribers should "always see producer updates" and "avoid data duplication," a scheduled export or Storage Transfer Service workflow is wrong.

The linked dataset is a read-only pointer to the publisher's shared dataset, so data is not copied into the subscriber project. This is the answer when the question says "zero-copy," "many subscribers," "data marketplace," "partner sharing," "cross-organization," "central producer controls access," or "discoverable curated data products."

Exam trap: if the prompt says subscribers should "always see producer updates" and "avoid data duplication," a scheduled export or Storage Transfer Service workflow is wrong.

read_content.heading: Analytics Hub Pub/Sub Sharing
read_content.body:
Analytics Hub can also publish Pub/Sub topics as listings so subscribers can consume streaming events through managed sharing. This appears when the scenario shifts from analytical tables to event streams: "share clickstream events with partner teams," "subscribe to near-real-time operational events," or "central producer publishes topics to many consumers." The important distinction is that the shared object is a Pub/Sub topic listing, not a BigQuery linked dataset.

Choose this when the consumer needs event delivery rather than ad hoc SQL over historical tables. Pub/Sub gives asynchronous fan-out, durable message retention for a configured period, and independent subscribers. Analytics Hub adds discovery, listing access, and exchange governance around the topic.

Pub/Sub topic listing vs BigQuery dataset listing: use topic listings for streams, dataset listings for analytical queryable data. Pub/Sub vs Dataflow: Pub/Sub transports events; Dataflow transforms or routes streams. Do not choose Dataflow just to "share" a stream if no transformation is required. Trap: BigQuery streaming inserts are for loading events into BigQuery; they are not the right answer when external subscribers need their own event subscription and the prompt emphasizes event distribution.

Analytics Hub can also publish Pub/Sub topics as listings so subscribers can consume streaming events through managed sharing. This appears when the scenario shifts from analytical tables to event streams: "share clickstream events with partner teams," "subscribe to near-real-time operational events," or "central producer publishes topics to many consumers." The important distinction is that the shared object is a Pub/Sub topic listing, not a BigQuery linked dataset.

Choose this when the consumer needs event delivery rather than ad hoc SQL over historical tables. Pub/Sub gives asynchronous fan-out, durable message retention for a configured period, and independent subscribers. Analytics Hub adds discovery, listing access, and exchange governance around the topic.

Pub/Sub vs Dataflow: Pub/Sub transports events; Dataflow transforms or routes streams. Do not choose Dataflow just to "share" a stream if no transformation is required. Trap: BigQuery streaming inserts are for loading events into BigQuery; they are not the right answer when external subscribers need their own event subscription and the prompt emphasizes event distribution.

read_content.heading: Linked Datasets
read_content.body:
A linked dataset is created in the subscriber project when a user subscribes to an Analytics Hub BigQuery listing. It behaves like a read-only dataset reference to the publisher's data. The subscriber can query the shared tables using BigQuery, but cannot mutate publisher data through the linked dataset. This preserves a single producer-owned copy while letting consumers work in their own projects.

Use linked datasets when the question emphasizes subscriber autonomy without ingestion: consumers use their own billing project for queries, keep local references, and receive publisher updates without scheduled loads. Linked datasets fit data mesh and data product scenarios because the provider owns publication and consumers discover and attach to products.

Linked dataset vs copied dataset: linked means no duplicate storage and fresher data; copied means separate lifecycle, possible drift, and extra governance. Linked dataset vs external table: external tables query data in external storage; linked datasets are Analytics Hub subscription artifacts for shared BigQuery data. Exam trap: linked datasets are not editable local replicas. If the question requires the subscriber to transform and own a derived dataset, the answer may include creating downstream tables from the linked data, but not changing the linked dataset itself.
