read_content.heading: IAM Inheritance And Least Privilege
read_content.body:
IAM is part of workload organization because access granted too high in the hierarchy leaks across workloads. An allow policy on the organization or folder inherits to child folders and projects. That is efficient for platform-wide roles such as monitoring viewers or security auditors, but risky for data access or production administration. For PDE, grant broad viewer/auditor roles high only when the scenario says centralized governance; grant data and job execution roles at the narrowest useful level.
Use project-level IAM for workload operators, dataset-level IAM for BigQuery dataset access, table/view-level controls for narrower analytics access, and policy tags for column-level sensitive fields. Separate service accounts by workload so Dataflow, Composer, dbt, and BI tools do not share one overprivileged identity.
X vs Authorized Views: IAM grants direct access; authorized views expose approved query results without giving base-table access. X vs row access policies: row policies filter rows; policy tags and column-level security restrict columns.
Trade-off: very granular IAM improves least privilege but raises operational complexity. Use groups rather than individual users for stable administration.
Exam trap: do not grant BigQuery Admin at the organization or folder to let analysts query data. For analysts, grant job creation in a query project and dataset/table access only where needed.

read_content.heading: Organization Policies Enforce Business Rules
read_content.body:
Organization Policy Service is the exam answer when a rule must be enforced across projects rather than checked after the fact. Common data-engineering constraints include restricting resource locations, requiring uniform bucket-level access, preventing public access, disabling service account key creation, limiting allowed APIs, requiring Shielded VM for supporting compute, or constraining CMEK and external sharing patterns. Attach the policy at the organization, folder, or project level that matches the business scope.
Use org policy for "must not create resources outside region X," "all prod projects must use CMEK," "disable service account key creation," or "prevent external identities from being added." Pair org policy with IAM and project hierarchy: folders define scope, org policy defines the non-negotiable rule, IAM defines who can administer.
Trade-off: org policies prevent drift but can block legitimate exceptions. Use folder structure to isolate exception zones instead of weakening policy globally.
X vs Cloud Monitoring: org policy blocks noncompliant creation; Monitoring detects behavior after it happens. X vs labels: labels describe intent; org policies enforce allowed states.
Exam trap: if a question says "ensure all future projects comply," do not pick a one-time audit, documentation, or manual review. Pick org policy at the appropriate folder or organization node.

read_content.heading: BigQuery Editions And Capacity Organization
read_content.body:
BigQuery workload organization often means separating compute capacity, not copying data. BigQuery Editions provide the commercial/capability model, while reservations allocate slots to workloads. The exam rarely asks price math; it asks which control prevents one workload from starving another. For committed or predictable analytics, create reservations such as bi-interactive, etl-batch, data-science, and background-maintenance, then assign projects or folders to those reservations.
Use Standard for simpler workloads, Enterprise for enterprise analytics features and autoscaling patterns, and Enterprise Plus where the scenario implies the highest availability/security/governance tier. The exact edition matters less than the principle: capacity isolation comes from reservations and assignments, not from table partitioning or dataset IAM.
Numeric anchors: BigQuery capacity is measured in slots. Reservations can have baseline slots and autoscaling max slots. Idle slots can be shared within compatible reservations, but idle capacity is preemptible and should not be the only capacity source for strict production SLAs.
Trade-off: dedicated baseline slots improve predictability but can lower utilization if idle-slot sharing is disabled. Autoscaling improves elasticity but has a max cap and still needs workload assignment.
Exam trap: "batch priority" does not create guaranteed capacity. If the requirement says BI dashboards must never be impacted by ETL, use separate reservations and assignments.

read_content.heading: Reservation Assignments Decide Who Uses Which Slots
read_content.body:
A BigQuery reservation alone is just a pool. A reservation assignment binds an organization, folder, or project to that pool for a job type. Job types include QUERY for SQL queries, PIPELINE for load/extract/copy pipeline jobs, ML_EXTERNAL for certain BigQuery ML external model jobs, BACKGROUND for background management work, and CONTINUOUS for continuous queries. A project can effectively use the closest applicable assignment in the hierarchy for a given job type.
Choose project-level assignments when one workload or team needs a known pool. Choose folder-level assignments when all projects under a business unit or environment should share capacity. Assign BI projects to a low-latency reservation and ETL projects to a separate reservation so ad hoc analysts cannot consume slots needed for scheduled transformations.
Trade-off: too many tiny reservations fragment capacity; too few reservations create noisy-neighbor risk. Enable idle-slot sharing when utilization matters, but reserve baseline capacity for strict jobs.
X vs quotas: reservations control performance isolation for slot-backed work; quotas cap usage. A team can stay within quota and still starve another team if both share one reservation.
Exam trap: assigning all projects to one default reservation is not workload isolation. The giveaway phrase is "must not affect" or "guaranteed capacity for each team."

read_content.heading: Interactive And Batch Query Priority
read_content.body:
BigQuery query priority is a scheduling hint for jobs, not a billing discount. INTERACTIVE is the default and is appropriate for dashboards, exploratory SQL, user-facing reports, and workloads that should start as soon as capacity is available. BATCH queues non-urgent queries and runs when resources are available; it is useful for backfills, scheduled aggregations, and overnight transformations where minutes or hours of delay are acceptable.
Use BATCH when the scenario says "non-urgent," "overnight," "backfill," "cost is same but do not compete with users," or "run when resources are available." Use INTERACTIVE for ad hoc analyst queries and BI tools where humans are waiting. If the scenario also says strict completion deadline, BATCH alone is insufficient; combine scheduled jobs with a reservation sized for the deadline.
Numeric anchor: batch jobs can wait before starting and are designed for non-time-critical work. Billing is still based on the pricing model: bytes processed for on-demand or slot usage/capacity under Editions.
X vs reservations: priority influences scheduling; reservations provide capacity boundaries. Priority cannot isolate two teams if they share the same capacity pool.
Exam trap: wrong answers often say "use batch because it is cheaper." The correct reason is lower urgency and reduced pressure on interactive workloads, not lower query price.

read_content.heading: Quotas Are Hard Guardrails
read_content.body:
Quotas organize workloads by placing hard limits on resource consumption. The exam uses phrases like "must not exceed 10 TiB queried per day," "prevent a tenant from running unlimited queries," or "stop one project from consuming all API capacity." For BigQuery on-demand queries, custom query quotas can cap bytes processed per project per day or per user per day. A query that would exceed the custom quota is rejected before it runs.
Use Service Usage quotas and BigQuery custom quotas for hard caps. Use project boundaries so each team or tenant has independent quota controls. Monitor quota utilization with Cloud Monitoring and audit quota errors through logs. Request quota increases when the requirement is legitimate growth, not containment.
Numeric anchors: BigQuery custom query quotas include QueryUsagePerDay at project level and QueryUsagePerUserPerDay at user level. Daily quotas reset on a daily schedule. Query usage quotas apply to on-demand query pricing; slot reservations control capacity instead.
X vs budgets: quotas stop usage; budgets alert. X vs reservations: quotas cap total consumption; reservations allocate compute capacity.
Exam trap: if the wording says "must not exceed," "block," or "prevent," budget alerts are not enough. Use quotas or an automated enforcement workflow.

read_content.heading: Budgets, Billing Export, Labels, And Chargeback
read_content.body:
Cost organization has two layers: attribution and control. Attribution uses projects, labels, tags, SKUs, folders, billing accounts, and Cloud Billing export to BigQuery. Control uses budgets for alerts, quotas for hard usage limits, and reservations/commitments for predictable BigQuery spend. For PDE questions, the most exam-relevant answer is usually "enable Cloud Billing export to BigQuery and require labels" when finance needs chargeback/showback.
Use labels such as team, app, environment, cost_center, data_domain, and owner on resources that support labels. Enforce labeling through project factory automation, deployment policy, or organization process. Use the billing export dataset for reports in BigQuery or Looker Studio. For BigQuery-specific slot attribution, job metadata such as reservation_id and project_id helps map consumption to teams.
Numeric anchors: budgets commonly alert at threshold percentages, but they do not automatically stop service usage. Billing export can include detailed SKU-level cost data suitable for daily or hourly analysis depending on export configuration.
X vs project boundary: labels improve reporting; projects improve billing and quota separation. Use both for clean chargeback.
Exam trap: "set a budget" is wrong for hard caps. "Add labels only" is incomplete when teams need separate quota, IAM, or blast-radius isolation.

read_content.heading: Data Sharing Without Copying Workloads
read_content.body:
Organizing analytics workloads does not mean duplicating every dataset into each team's project. For governed sharing, keep source data in controlled producer projects and expose it through BigQuery IAM, authorized views, row access policies, column-level security, or Analytics Hub listings. Consumers can run jobs from their own projects, which keeps query cost and quotas attributed to the consuming team while avoiding stale copies.
Use Analytics Hub when the scenario says "share curated datasets with many internal or external consumers," "data exchange," or "avoid copying data." Use authorized views when consumers need a restricted logical view. Use row access policies for tenant or region row filtering. Use policy tags for sensitive columns such as SSN, email, or credit card fields.
Trade-off: sharing central data reduces duplication and governance drift but requires clear producer/consumer IAM. Copying can improve isolation for experiments but creates freshness, lineage, and compliance risks.
X vs VPC Service Controls: sharing controls who can query data; VPC Service Controls reduce data exfiltration risk around service perimeters.
Exam trap: a common distractor says "export and copy the tables into each department project." If the requirement says freshness, governance, or minimal operations, use governed sharing and charge query jobs to consumer projects.

read_content.heading: Environment Separation And Promotion
read_content.body:
Prod, staging, and dev should normally be separate projects, often under environment folders or team folders with environment suffixes. This lets production have stricter IAM, org policies, quotas, budgets, service accounts, and change control. It also prevents developers from accidentally modifying production datasets, Pub/Sub topics, Dataflow jobs, or Composer environments.
Use separate projects when the scenario mentions different release cadence, lower-trust experimentation, separate datasets, separate service accounts, or independent quota/billing. Use CI/CD to promote definitions from dev to prod rather than granting humans broad prod access. For BigQuery, use separate datasets or projects for dev tables, and avoid granting dev service accounts write access to prod datasets.
Trade-off: separate projects increase deployment and networking setup. Shared VPC and centralized logging can reduce operational overhead while preserving isolation.
X vs dataset prefixing: naming datasets dev_sales and prod_sales inside one project is weaker than project separation because IAM, quotas, APIs, and service accounts still share a project boundary.
Exam trap: if the question says "minimize blast radius" or "developers must not affect production," do not choose only labels, naming conventions, or dataset prefixes. Use separate projects and inherited production controls.

This lets production have stricter IAM, org policies, quotas, budgets, service accounts, and change control. Use separate projects when the scenario mentions different release cadence, lower-trust experimentation, separate datasets, separate service accounts, or independent quota/billing. Exam trap: if the question says "minimize blast radius" or "developers must not affect production," do not choose only labels, naming conventions, or dataset prefixes. Use separate projects and inherited production controls.

read_content.heading: Latency And SLA Drive Workload Placement
read_content.body:
Business requirements often hide an SLA. "Dashboard must load in seconds," "fraud detection under 1 second," "nightly batch by 6 AM," and "monthly backfill can take hours" are different workload classes. Organize them into services and capacity pools that match latency. BigQuery plus reservations works for SQL analytics and dashboards. Dataflow streaming handles low-latency event processing. Pub/Sub decouples producers and consumers. Cloud Bigtable handles high-throughput low-latency key-value access. Spanner handles globally consistent relational transactions.
Use BigQuery BI Engine or materialized views when dashboard acceleration is the issue. Use reservation separation when dashboard capacity is being stolen by ETL. Use Dataflow when the requirement is event-time processing, windows, and exactly-once-style stream processing. Use Pub/Sub when ingestion spikes must be absorbed before processing.
Trade-off: over-isolating every SLA class into dedicated capacity improves predictability but raises cost. Use shared idle capacity for lower-priority work, not for strict deadlines.
X vs batch priority: BATCH is acceptable for no-SLA backfills; it is risky for a 6 AM business-critical report unless reservation capacity is sized.
Exam trap: do not answer every performance problem with partitioning. Partitioning reduces scanned data; reservations and service choice address compute contention and latency guarantees.

Pub/Sub decouples producers and consumers. Cloud Bigtable handles high-throughput low-latency key-value access. Spanner handles globally consistent relational transactions. read_content.heading: Operational Ownership And No-Ops Preferences
read_content.body:
The PDE exam values managed services when the business requirement says low operational overhead. For workload organization, this means using BigQuery reservations instead of self-managed query clusters, Dataflow instead of hand-built streaming workers, Pub/Sub instead of custom queues, Cloud Composer only when workflow orchestration is needed, and managed exports/Monitoring instead of custom billing scrapers.
Choose no-ops controls first: folder/project hierarchy, org policies, IAM groups, quotas, budgets, billing export, reservations, and managed sharing. Use Terraform or project factory patterns for repeatability, but the exam answer is usually the GCP control, not "write a custom script," unless the question explicitly needs automation beyond built-in capability.
Trade-off: managed controls are less bespoke but easier to audit and operate. Custom automation can enforce cross-service hard spend caps, but it adds failure modes and is not the first answer when quotas or org policies exist.
X vs Cloud Functions enforcement: budget-alert-to-Pub/Sub-to-Function can disable billing or APIs as a custom pattern, but it is a workaround, not the default answer for BigQuery query caps.
Exam trap: avoid self-managed Hadoop/Spark clusters for simple SQL analytics workload separation. BigQuery reservations and quotas are the exam-native controls.

read_content.heading: Observability Confirms Workload Design
read_content.body:
After organizing workloads, measure whether the boundary works. For BigQuery, inspect job history, reservation usage, slot utilization, queued time, bytes processed, user/project labels, and failures. INFORMATION_SCHEMA views and audit logs show which projects and users ran jobs, what reservations were used, and how much data was processed. Cloud Monitoring shows quota usage, reservation metrics, and alert conditions. Cloud Billing export shows cost by SKU, project, folder, and label.
Use observability when the prompt says "identify which team caused the spike," "allocate cost to departments," "alert when usage nears quota," or "prove compliance." Use log-based metrics or Monitoring alert policies for operational thresholds. Use billing export for finance reports, not for millisecond operational control.
Trade-off: billing data can lag and is best for reporting; job metadata is better for near-real-time BigQuery operations. Logs are detailed but need retention and routing design.
X vs enforcement: Monitoring and billing reports do not stop usage. Pair them with quotas, reservations, IAM, or org policies when prevention is required.
Exam trap: a question that asks to "prevent recurrence" after diagnosing a noisy neighbor needs a control change, not just a dashboard. Diagnose with logs/metrics; fix with reservations, assignments, quotas, or project separation.
read_content.body_html:
After organizing workloads, measure whether the boundary works. For BigQuery, inspect job history, reservation usage, slot utilization, queued time, bytes processed, user/project labels, and failures. INFORMATION_SCHEMA views and audit logs show which projects and users ran jobs, what reservations were used, and how much data was processed. Cloud Monitoring shows quota usage, reservation metrics, and alert conditions. Cloud Billing export shows cost by SKU, project, folder, and label. Use observability when the prompt says "identify which team caused the spike," "allocate cost to departments," "alert when usage nears quota," or "prove compliance." Use log-based metrics or Monitoring alert policies for operational thresholds. Use billing export for finance reports, not for millisecond operational control.

Trade-off: billing data can lag and is best for reporting; job metadata is better for near-real-time BigQuery operations. Logs are detailed but need retention and routing design.

X vs enforcement: Monitoring and billing reports do not stop usage. Pair them with quotas, reservations, IAM, or org policies when prevention is required.

Exam trap: a question that asks to "prevent recurrence" after diagnosing a noisy neighbor needs a control change, not just a dashboard. Diagnose with logs/metrics; fix with reservations, assignments, quotas, or project separation.

read_content.heading: Least Disruptive Lever Wins
read_content.body:
Many exam choices are technically possible but overbuilt. The right answer is usually the narrowest GCP-native control that satisfies the explicit requirement. If analysts slow ETL, create separate BigQuery reservations and assignments; do not redesign the whole data lake. If finance needs team cost reports, enable billing export and labels; do not split every dataset. If dev can affect prod, separate projects and IAM; do not rely on naming. If EU projects must stay in EU, attach an org policy to the EU folder.
Use a decision ladder: first identify the requirement type: isolation, governance, cost visibility, hard cap, latency, or sharing. Then choose the control plane: hierarchy, IAM/org policy, labels/billing export, quotas, reservations, or Analytics Hub. Then scope it: org, folder, project, dataset, table, user, or reservation.
Trade-off: broad controls are simpler to reason about but can block unrelated teams. Narrow controls reduce blast radius but require more administration.
X vs migration: replatforming to another service is rarely needed if the issue is only ownership, quota, or capacity allocation.
Exam trap: distractors often choose a powerful service for the wrong axis, such as Spanner for workload isolation, Dataproc for BigQuery contention, or budgets for hard prevention.

Exam trap: distractors often choose a powerful service for the wrong axis, such as Spanner for workload isolation, Dataproc for BigQuery contention, or budgets for hard prevention.

choose_this_when: Separate projects - prod and dev must have different IAM, quotas, service accounts, audit scope, budgets, or blast radius.
Folders - the same IAM, organization policy, budget grouping, or delegated admin model must apply to many projects.
Organization Policy Service - the requirement says enforce, prohibit, restrict locations, require CMEK, disable service account keys, or ensure future projects comply.
BigQuery reservations - BI, ETL, ML, and ad hoc SQL must have predictable capacity or avoid noisy-neighbor slot contention.
Reservation assignments - capacity policy should apply to a specific project, folder, or job type such as QUERY or PIPELINE.
Batch query priority - non-urgent backfills, overnight transformations, or reports can wait for available resources.
Interactive query priority - humans or dashboards are waiting and the query should start promptly.
BigQuery custom query quotas - on-demand BigQuery usage must not exceed a project/user daily processed-bytes limit, such as 10 TiB/day.
Cloud Billing budgets - stakeholders need spend alerts at thresholds, not hard enforcement.
Cloud Billing export to BigQuery - finance wants chargeback/showback by project, SKU, folder, or labels.
Labels/tags - cost reports, automation, ownership, and inventory need consistent metadata across resources.
Analytics Hub - curated BigQuery datasets must be shared to many consumers without copying tables.
Authorized views/row access policies/policy tags - consumers need limited access to rows, columns, or derived views without base-table access.
Cloud Monitoring - teams need alerts for quota utilization, reservation saturation, job failures, or anomalous usage.
Consumer query projects - shared data should stay centralized but query cost/quota should be charged to the consuming team.
avoid_this_when: Single project for dev/stage/prod - avoid when the requirement mentions blast radius, production protection, separate IAM, or separate quotas.
One BigQuery reservation for BI and ETL - avoid when dashboards and scheduled pipelines must not compete for slots.
Budget alerts as hard caps - avoid when wording says prevent, block, must not exceed, or enforce.
Labels as isolation - avoid when access, quota, or policy boundaries are required; labels report but do not block.
Copying BigQuery tables to each department - avoid when freshness, governance, lineage, or minimal operations matters.
Batch priority for strict deadlines - avoid when the job must complete by a fixed SLA unless capacity is separately guaranteed.
Org-level broad IAM grants - avoid for analysts, developers, or workload service accounts that need only dataset/project access.
Dataset-only separation - avoid when teams need separate billing, query quotas, APIs, service accounts, or production controls.
Idle slots as the only production capacity - avoid when the workload has strict completion or latency requirements.
Custom scripts for cost caps - avoid when BigQuery custom quotas or service quotas satisfy the stated cap.
Deep folder hierarchies - avoid when naming and labels would handle reporting without extra inherited-policy complexity.
Changing storage design to fix compute contention - avoid when the real problem is reservation assignment or query priority.
Using Dataproc for basic BigQuery workload isolation - avoid when the workload is SQL analytics already suited to BigQuery.
Granting BigQuery Admin to solve query access - avoid when jobUser plus dataset/table access is sufficient.
common_traps: "Set a budget to prevent overspend" is wrong when the giveaway is must not exceed, block, enforce, or hard cap; budgets alert only.
"Use batch priority because it is cheaper" is wrong; batch is a scheduling choice for non-urgent work, not a pricing discount.
"Put all teams in one reservation and rely on fair scheduling" is wrong when the prompt requires guaranteed isolation or no impact between teams.
"Use labels to isolate workloads" is wrong; labels support reporting and automation, not IAM, quotas, or slot guarantees.
"Copy datasets into every team's project" is wrong when the prompt emphasizes freshness, governance, lineage, or minimal operations.
"Grant BigQuery Admin so analysts can query" is wrong; use jobUser on query project plus dataset/table/view access.
"Use one project with dataset prefixes for dev and prod" is wrong when blast radius, IAM, quotas, service accounts, or audit separation matters.
"Use Monitoring alerts to enforce compliance" is wrong when future resource creation must be prevented; use Organization Policy Service.
"Rely only on idle slots for production deadline jobs" is wrong because idle slots can be preempted.
"Partition or cluster tables to stop noisy neighbors" is incomplete; partitioning reduces scanned data but does not isolate slot capacity.
"Move SQL analytics to Dataproc to control capacity" is usually wrong when BigQuery reservations solve the stated problem with lower operations.
"Assign IAM at organization for convenience" is risky when the workload is a specific team, dataset, or project; inheritance broadens access.
"Use a folder only for visual organization" misses the exam point; folders are policy and delegation scopes.
"Use quotas for reservation-backed performance isolation" confuses caps with capacity; quotas limit usage but do not guarantee slots.
"Use reservations to cap on-demand bytes processed" confuses capacity allocation with hard query-usage quotas.

clue_words.phrase: ad hoc analysts slow nightly ETL
clue_words.answer: Separate BigQuery reservations and reservation assignments

clue_words.phrase: dashboards must not be impacted by batch jobs
clue_words.answer: Dedicated BI reservation plus separate ETL reservation

clue_words.phrase: non-urgent backfill
human waiting for query results
must not exceed 10 TiB queried per day
alert when monthly spend reaches 90 percent
chargeback by department
clue_words.answer: Cloud Billing export to BigQuery plus labels/projects

clue_words.phrase: showback by cost center
clue_words.answer: Labels or tags plus billing export

clue_words.phrase: prod isolated from dev
apply same policy to all production projects
clue_words.answer: Production folder with inherited IAM/org policies

clue_words.phrase: all EU data workloads must stay in europe-west regions
clue_words.answer: Organization Policy Service allowed resource locations at EU folder

clue_words.phrase: disable service account key creation everywhere
clue_words.answer: Organization Policy Service constraint at organization/folder

clue_words.phrase: central team shares curated datasets without copies
consumers pay for their own queries against shared data
clue_words.answer: Consumer query projects with access to producer datasets

clue_words.phrase: only expose approved columns
clue_words.answer: BigQuery policy tags or authorized views

clue_words.phrase: only expose approved rows
enforce CMEK for regulated projects
clue_words.answer: Organization Policy Service plus regulated folder

clue_words.phrase: one team should not consume all slots
identify which team caused a cost spike

clue_words.phrase: delegated administration for a business unit
clue_words.answer: Folder-level IAM on that business-unit folder

decision_table.if_question_says: Critical batch must not be slowed by ad-hoc analysts

service_decision_table.if_question_says: ETL must not be slowed by ad hoc analysts
service_decision_table.answer: Create separate BigQuery reservations and assign ETL and analyst projects/folders separately.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Dashboards must remain responsive while batch jobs run
service_decision_table.answer: Use dedicated BI reservation for dashboard/query projects and separate ETL reservation for pipelines.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Backfill can run whenever resources are free
service_decision_table.answer: Set BigQuery query priority to BATCH.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Analyst is waiting for SQL result in a BI tool
service_decision_table.answer: Use INTERACTIVE priority and assign the BI project to appropriate capacity.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Team must not query more than 10 TiB/day on on-demand BigQuery
service_decision_table.answer: Set BigQuery custom query quota such as QueryUsagePerDay or QueryUsagePerUserPerDay.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Notify owner when spend reaches 50, 90, and 100 percent
service_decision_table.answer: Use Cloud Billing budgets and alerts.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Stop usage after a spend threshold
service_decision_table.answer: Use service-specific quotas where available; budget alerts alone do not stop usage.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Finance wants cost by team and application
service_decision_table.answer: Use projects plus labels/tags and Cloud Billing export to BigQuery.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Production must be isolated from development
service_decision_table.answer: Use separate projects, preferably under prod/non-prod folders with inherited controls.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: All production projects need the same constraints
service_decision_table.answer: Attach IAM and Organization Policy Service constraints at the production folder.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Restrict all regulated workloads to approved regions
service_decision_table.answer: Use Organization Policy Service resource location constraints on the regulated folder.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Prevent service account key creation across a folder
service_decision_table.answer: Use Organization Policy Service constraint scoped to that folder.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Multiple teams need fresh curated BigQuery data without copies
service_decision_table.answer: Use Analytics Hub or authorized datasets/views.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Consumers should pay for queries against producer data
service_decision_table.answer: Let consumers run jobs from their own query projects with access to producer datasets.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Only certain columns can be visible to analysts
service_decision_table.answer: Use BigQuery policy tags/column-level security or authorized views.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Only certain rows can be visible per region or tenant
Central admins need visibility into all assets and IAM
service_decision_table.answer: Use Cloud Asset Inventory plus appropriate inherited viewer/auditor IAM.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need alert when quota nears exhaustion
service_decision_table.answer: Use Cloud Monitoring alerting on quota metrics and logs.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need delegated administration for a department
service_decision_table.answer: Create a department folder and grant folder-level IAM to the admin group.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need low-ops SQL analytics capacity isolation
service_decision_table.answer: Use BigQuery Editions, reservations, and assignments rather than self-managed clusters.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need strict 6 AM completion for batch report
service_decision_table.answer: Use scheduled query/pipeline plus reservation capacity sized for deadline; batch priority alone is not enough.

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need metadata for ownership and cost automation
service_decision_table.answer: Use labels/tags consistently, enforced through provisioning policy or project factory.

scenario_patterns.how_question_disguises_it: The prompt says analysts run ad hoc SQL during the day, BI dashboards time out, or nightly pipelines miss deadlines after another team starts large queries. The answer is to split workloads into BigQuery reservations and assign projects/folders/job types appropriately; use batch priority only for non-urgent jobs and quotas only for caps.

The story mentions a team, tenant, or project must not process more than a fixed amount, such as 10 TiB/day. The correct control is a quota, especially BigQuery custom query quotas for on-demand bytes processed; budget alerts are only notification.

The company wants monthly cost by department, app, environment, or cost center. Use project boundaries, mandatory labels/tags, and Cloud Billing export to BigQuery; do not build a custom scraper or rely only on console screenshots.

Developers accidentally affect production or non-prod tests consume shared quotas. Use separate projects for prod and non-prod under folders with different IAM/org policies; dataset prefixes inside one project are too weak.

Every project in a division or regulatory zone must follow the same location, CMEK, public-access, or service-account-key rule. Use folders to scope the set and Organization Policy Service to enforce the constraint.

Multiple teams need fresh governed access to the same curated BigQuery datasets and copies are causing drift. Use Analytics Hub or authorized datasets/views so producers govern data and consumers query from their own projects.

Analysts need to query only certain columns, rows, or aggregated results from sensitive tables. Use policy tags, row access policies, authorized views, and narrow IAM; do not grant BigQuery Admin or copy sanitized tables manually.

The company wants to organize capacity and cost without running clusters. Prefer BigQuery reservations, quotas, budgets, billing export, Monitoring, and org policies over Dataproc or custom schedulers unless the workload itself requires Spark/Hadoop.

Some jobs are user-facing and need seconds, while others are overnight or monthly. Put interactive dashboards on interactive priority/reserved capacity and backfills on batch priority or lower-priority reservations; do not treat all SQL jobs the same.

A platform team owns the org while business units manage their own analytics projects. Use folder-level delegation, project factory patterns, inherited guardrails, and per-team billing/quota boundaries.

exam_pattern_notes: If the wording says hard cap, must not exceed, prevent, or block, choose quotas or org-policy-style enforcement, not budgets or dashboards.
If the wording says alert, notify, report, showback, or trend, choose budgets, Cloud Monitoring, labels, and billing export.
If BigQuery workloads interfere with each other, choose reservations and assignments before changing storage layout or services.
If the workload is non-urgent BigQuery SQL, choose BATCH priority; if a human or dashboard waits, choose INTERACTIVE priority.
If the question mentions batch priority as a cost-saving mechanism, reject it; priority changes scheduling, not the fundamental pricing model.
If prod and dev have different risk, IAM, quota, or lifecycle, separate projects are the default answer.
If the same rule applies to many projects, use folders plus inherited IAM or Organization Policy Service, not repeated manual project settings.
If the requirement is chargeback, use project structure plus labels and Cloud Billing export to BigQuery; labels alone are incomplete for strong separation.
If shared data must remain fresh and governed, use Analytics Hub, authorized views, row policies, or policy tags instead of copying tables.
If consumers must pay for shared BigQuery data access, have them run jobs in their own projects while accessing producer datasets.

If PII and BigQuery appear together, decide whether the requirement is restrict access, mask/de-identify data, or discover/classify data; policy tags restrict columns, DLP de-identifies, Data Catalog aids discovery.
If a service account is used by multiple workloads, split identities by workload before granting broad roles; this improves audit and blast radius.
If the exam says low operational overhead, prefer managed controls such as BigQuery reservations, quotas, org policies, Monitoring, and billing export over custom schedulers or clusters.

If strict latency is under one second for serving lookups, BigQuery workload organization is not enough; consider Bigtable or Spanner depending on access pattern and consistency needs, while still using projects/folders for governance.
final_review_bullets: Project = billing, quota, IAM, API, service-account, and audit boundary.
Folder = inherited IAM/org-policy/delegation boundary for many projects.
Labels/tags help chargeback; they do not enforce isolation.
Budgets alert; quotas block.
BigQuery custom query quotas cap on-demand processed bytes per project/day or user/day.
BigQuery reservations isolate slot capacity between BI, ETL, ML, and ad hoc workloads.
Reservation assignment binds capacity to org/folder/project plus job type.
Batch priority is for non-urgent jobs; it is not cheaper by itself.
Interactive priority is default for user-facing SQL and dashboards.
Separate prod and dev into separate projects when blast radius matters.
Use org policy for future-proof enforcement such as allowed locations or CMEK requirements.
Use billing export to BigQuery for cost reports and chargeback.
Use Analytics Hub or authorized views to share data without copying tables.
Consumer query projects keep shared storage centralized while charging query cost to consumers.
Idle slots improve utilization but are preemptible; do not rely on them for strict SLAs.
One default reservation is not workload isolation.

5.4 Monitoring and troubleshooting proce.what_the_exam_tests: This section tests whether you can map an operational symptom to the right observability signal, then pick the lowest-overhead remediation path. Expect scenario questions that describe lag, quota errors, slot contention, cost spikes, hot keys, audit gaps, or failed jobs and ask which metric, log, view, dashboard, or command you should use first. The exam rewards service-specific diagnosis: Dataflow lag is not worker CPU, BigQuery cost is not only Cloud Billing, and Bigtable latency is often key distribution, not generic node count.
must_know_services: Cloud Monitoring - central metrics, dashboards, SLOs, uptime checks, and alerting policies for managed service signals.
Cloud Logging - structured logs, audit logs, log queries, log sinks, and log-based metrics when no native metric exists.
Error Reporting - aggregates application exceptions and stack traces from supported runtimes; useful for repeated code failures, not data-plane throughput.
Cloud Trace - distributed latency traces for request paths; use when the symptom is per-request latency across services.
BigQuery - exposes job metadata, query history, slot usage, reservations, BI Engine metrics, and audit logs for cost and performance diagnosis.
BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT - near real-time job metadata and 180-day job history for finding expensive, slow, failed, or unpartitioned queries.
BigQuery admin panel - Capacity tab shows slot utilization, reservation assignment, queueing, and capacity recommendations.
BigQuery Reservations - manages dedicated slots, editions capacity, assignments, autoscaling, and workload isolation.
Pub/Sub - subscription backlog metrics identify subscriber or downstream processing bottlenecks before messages expire.
Dataflow - job graph, worker logs, counters, watermarks, system_lag, and data_freshness diagnose streaming backpressure and stuck stages.
Cloud Composer - Airflow DAG/task logs, retries, scheduler health, and environment metrics diagnose orchestration failures.
Bigtable - node CPU, storage utilization, latency metrics, and Key Visualizer diagnose hot tablets and poor row-key design.
Bigtable Key Visualizer - heatmap of read/write activity by key range; first tool for hot-row and uneven traffic scenarios.
Cloud SQL - CPU, memory, disk, connections, query insights, and slow query logs for relational workload troubleshooting.
Cloud Billing export to BigQuery - durable cost and usage analytics for project, label, SKU, and service cost investigations.
Cloud Audit Logs - Admin Activity, Data Access, System Event, and Policy Denied logs for who did what and whether access was denied.
Cloud Storage - request logs, audit logs, object lifecycle state, retention policy, and 4xx/5xx metrics for ingestion and archival issues.
Dataproc - YARN/Spark driver logs, autoscaling events, and cluster metrics for Hadoop/Spark job failures.
Managed Service for Prometheus - collection for Prometheus-style metrics from GKE or hybrid workloads when PDE scenarios include custom exporters.

read_content.heading: Observability Decision Model
read_content.body:
Start with the failure mode, not the product menu. The PDE exam usually gives a symptom and expects a matching signal: backlog, freshness, slot contention, cost spike, quota error, job failure, audit need, or latency spike.
- Concept: metrics answer "is it broken and how badly," logs answer "what happened," traces answer "where did one request spend time," and metadata views answer "which job/query/user/table caused it."
- When to use: use Cloud Monitoring for numeric alerting, Cloud Logging for event investigation and audit, INFORMATION_SCHEMA for BigQuery job forensics, and service consoles when the managed service has a purpose-built view such as Bigtable Key Visualizer or Dataflow job graph.
- Trade-off: high-cardinality logs are powerful but expensive to ingest and query; metrics are cheap and alert-friendly but lose detailed context. Export logs or billing data to BigQuery when questions become analytical, recurring, or multi-day.
- Exam trap: do not select generic VM metrics when the managed data service has a semantic metric. Dataflow streaming lag is system_lag/data_freshness, Pub/Sub backlog is oldest_unacked_message_age/num_undelivered_messages, and BigQuery cost attribution is JOBS_BY_PROJECT plus billing export.
read_content.body_html:
Start with the failure mode, not the product menu. The PDE exam usually gives a symptom and expects a matching signal: backlog, freshness, slot contention, cost spike, quota error, job failure, audit need, or latency spike.

  • Concept: metrics answer "is it broken and how badly," logs answer "what happened," traces answer "where did one request spend time," and metadata views answer "which job/query/user/table caused it."
  • When to use: use Cloud Monitoring for numeric alerting, Cloud Logging for event investigation and audit, INFORMATION_SCHEMA for BigQuery job forensics, and service consoles when the managed service has a purpose-built view such as Bigtable Key Visualizer or Dataflow job graph.
  • Trade-off: high-cardinality logs are powerful but expensive to ingest and query; metrics are cheap and alert-friendly but lose detailed context. Export logs or billing data to BigQuery when questions become analytical, recurring, or multi-day.
  • Exam trap: do not select generic VM metrics when the managed data service has a semantic metric. Dataflow streaming lag is system_lag/data_freshness, Pub/Sub backlog is oldest_unacked_message_age/num_undelivered_messages, and BigQuery cost attribution is JOBS_BY_PROJECT plus billing export.

read_content.heading: Cloud Monitoring Metrics and Alerts
read_content.body:
Cloud Monitoring is the default answer for dashboards, alerting policies, notification channels, SLOs, uptime checks, and service metrics. Managed services publish metrics automatically, so the exam expects you to know the metric family and not build a custom poller.
- When to use: alert on user-impacting symptoms such as latency p95/p99, error ratio, backlog age, failed job count, slot utilization, disk nearing full, or unavailable service endpoints. Use metric absence when a pipeline should emit heartbeats and silence means failure.
- Trade-offs: alerts need an alignment period and duration to avoid flapping; page on sustained impact rather than a single error. Use grouping labels carefully because per-user/per-table/per-job cardinality can make dashboards noisy.
- Numeric anchors: Cloud Logging _Default retention is commonly 30 days; _Required audit log bucket retention is 400 days. BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA job history is 180 days. These numbers push long-term trend analysis toward exports to BigQuery or Cloud Storage.
- Exam trap: "notify within minutes if streaming is falling behind" means Cloud Monitoring alert on lag/backlog metrics, not a scheduled query scanning logs every hour.
read_content.body_html:
Cloud Monitoring is the default answer for dashboards, alerting policies, notification channels, SLOs, uptime checks, and service metrics. Managed services publish metrics automatically, so the exam expects you to know the metric family and not build a custom poller.

  • When to use: alert on user-impacting symptoms such as latency p95/p99, error ratio, backlog age, failed job count, slot utilization, disk nearing full, or unavailable service endpoints. Use metric absence when a pipeline should emit heartbeats and silence means failure.
  • Trade-offs: alerts need an alignment period and duration to avoid flapping; page on sustained impact rather than a single error. Use grouping labels carefully because per-user/per-table/per-job cardinality can make dashboards noisy.
  • Numeric anchors: Cloud Logging _Default retention is commonly 30 days; _Required audit log bucket retention is 400 days. BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA job history is 180 days. These numbers push long-term trend analysis toward exports to BigQuery or Cloud Storage.
  • Exam trap: "notify within minutes if streaming is falling behind" means Cloud Monitoring alert on lag/backlog metrics, not a scheduled query scanning logs every hour.

read_content.heading: Cloud Logging, Audit Logs, and Log-Based Metrics
read_content.body:
Cloud Logging stores structured service logs, application logs, and audit logs. It is the right investigation tool when the question includes error messages, stack traces, denied permissions, resource names, request payload summaries, or "who changed this."
- When to use: query logs to inspect Dataflow worker exceptions, Cloud Composer task logs, BigQuery audit events, Cloud SQL slow query logs, or Cloud Storage 403/404 spikes. Create log-based metrics when the failure pattern appears only in logs, such as a specific exception class, bad record counter, or external API error string.
- Trade-offs: log-based metrics are alertable in Cloud Monitoring, but they depend on log ingestion and pattern quality. Log sinks route logs to BigQuery for analytics, Cloud Storage for archive, or Pub/Sub for real-time processing.
- Audit nuance: Admin Activity, System Event, and Policy Denied logs are generally enabled by default. Data Access logs are often disabled by default because of volume, but BigQuery Data Access audit logs are a major exception and are important for query/read auditing.
- Exam trap: "alert when this exact error appears" is log-based metric plus alert policy. "Analyze six months of logs" is a log sink/export, not the Logs Explorer default view.
read_content.body_html:
Cloud Logging stores structured service logs, application logs, and audit logs. It is the right investigation tool when the question includes error messages, stack traces, denied permissions, resource names, request payload summaries, or "who changed this."

  • When to use: query logs to inspect Dataflow worker exceptions, Cloud Composer task logs, BigQuery audit events, Cloud SQL slow query logs, or Cloud Storage 403/404 spikes. Create log-based metrics when the failure pattern appears only in logs, such as a specific exception class, bad record counter, or external API error string.
  • Trade-offs: log-based metrics are alertable in Cloud Monitoring, but they depend on log ingestion and pattern quality. Log sinks route logs to BigQuery for analytics, Cloud Storage for archive, or Pub/Sub for real-time processing.
  • Audit nuance: Admin Activity, System Event, and Policy Denied logs are generally enabled by default. Data Access logs are often disabled by default because of volume, but BigQuery Data Access audit logs are a major exception and are important for query/read auditing.
  • Exam trap: "alert when this exact error appears" is log-based metric plus alert policy. "Analyze six months of logs" is a log sink/export, not the Logs Explorer default view.

read_content.heading: Metrics vs Logs vs Traces
read_content.body:
The exam frequently offers Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, Error Reporting, and Cloud Trace as similar-looking options. Pick by data shape.
- Metrics: numeric time series; best for thresholds, SLO burn, dashboards, anomaly detection, and fast alerting. Examples: Dataflow system_lag, Pub/Sub oldest_unacked_message_age, Bigtable server latency, BigQuery slot utilization.
- Logs: structured events; best for exact error text, audit trails, per-record failures, stack traces, denied IAM decisions, and postmortem context. Logs can become metrics through log-based metrics.
- Traces: request execution paths; best when a synchronous application request passes through Cloud Run, GKE, APIs, and databases and the question asks which component caused latency.
- Error Reporting: exception aggregation; best for repeated application exceptions, not pipeline throughput or cost.
- X vs Y: Cloud Trace explains request latency distribution across services; Cloud Monitoring alerts that latency is above threshold; Cloud Logging shows the failing request/error details.
- Exam trap: if the workload is batch SQL, Dataflow, Pub/Sub, or Bigtable, Cloud Trace is usually a distractor unless the scenario explicitly describes request spans or microservice latency.
read_content.body_html:
The exam frequently offers Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, Error Reporting, and Cloud Trace as similar-looking options. Pick by data shape.

  • Metrics: numeric time series; best for thresholds, SLO burn, dashboards, anomaly detection, and fast alerting. Examples: Dataflow system_lag, Pub/Sub oldest_unacked_message_age, Bigtable server latency, BigQuery slot utilization.
  • Logs: structured events; best for exact error text, audit trails, per-record failures, stack traces, denied IAM decisions, and postmortem context. Logs can become metrics through log-based metrics.
  • Traces: request execution paths; best when a synchronous application request passes through Cloud Run, GKE, APIs, and databases and the question asks which component caused latency.
  • Error Reporting: exception aggregation; best for repeated application exceptions, not pipeline throughput or cost.
  • X vs Y: Cloud Trace explains request latency distribution across services; Cloud Monitoring alerts that latency is above threshold; Cloud Logging shows the failing request/error details.
  • Exam trap: if the workload is batch SQL, Dataflow, Pub/Sub, or Bigtable, Cloud Trace is usually a distractor unless the scenario explicitly describes request spans or microservice latency.

read_content.heading: BigQuery Job Troubleshooting
read_content.body:
For BigQuery troubleshooting, use both the console and SQL metadata. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT (synonymous with JOBS at project scope) contains near real-time metadata for running jobs and completed job history for the past 180 days.
- When to use: find expensive queries, failed jobs, long-running jobs, users scanning the most bytes, queries missing partition filters, jobs by label, reservation usage, referenced tables, error_result, total_bytes_processed, total_slot_ms, cache_hit, and creation/end time.
- Trade-offs: INFORMATION_SCHEMA is region-scoped, so the query location must match the job location, such as region-us or region-eu. For cross-project or org investigations, use JOBS_BY_ORGANIZATION if you have the right IAM, or export/query per project and region.
- Cost angle: total_bytes_processed explains on-demand query cost; total_slot_ms explains capacity consumption and reservation pressure. Billing export provides invoice-grade cost by SKU/project/label, while JOBS views provide operational attribution by job/user/query.
- Exam trap: "which user caused the spike this morning" is JOBS_BY_PROJECT; "what will the invoice be by cost center for the month" is Cloud Billing export to BigQuery.
read_content.body_html:

  • Trade-offs: INFORMATION_SCHEMA is region-scoped, so the query location must match the job location, such as region-us or region-eu. For cross-project or org investigations, use JOBS_BY_ORGANIZATION if you have the right IAM, or export/query per project and region.

  • Exam trap: "which user caused the spike this morning" is JOBS_BY_PROJECT; "what will the invoice be by cost center for the month" is Cloud Billing export to BigQuery.

read_content.heading: BigQuery Slots, Reservations, and Capacity
read_content.body:
BigQuery performance questions often describe slow queries, queued jobs, reservation contention, or a message such as slot quota exceeded. The first stop is the BigQuery admin panel Capacity tab and reservation metrics, not adding indexes or scaling workers.
- When to use: inspect slot utilization, queued work, autoscaling behavior, reservation assignments, project consumption, and capacity recommendations. Use reservations to isolate workloads such as ETL, dashboards, and ad hoc analysts so one class cannot starve another.
- Trade-offs: on-demand pricing charges by bytes scanned and uses shared capacity; editions/reservations give predictable capacity and workload isolation but require capacity management. Flex-like temporary capacity patterns are useful for bursts; autoscaling can absorb spikes but still has configured limits.
- X vs Y: optimizing SQL/partitioning reduces bytes scanned and slot use; buying more slots treats capacity pressure without fixing waste. Use JOBS_BY_PROJECT to identify waste before increasing commitments.
- Exam trap: "queries are queued while slot utilization is saturated" points to reservation capacity or assignment changes. "Queries scanned too much data" points to partitioning, clustering, pruning, or query rewrite.
read_content.body_html:

  • When to use: inspect slot utilization, queued work, autoscaling behavior, reservation assignments, project consumption, and capacity recommendations. Use reservations to isolate workloads such as ETL, dashboards, and ad hoc analysts so one class cannot starve another.
  • Trade-offs: on-demand pricing charges by bytes scanned and uses shared capacity; editions/reservations give predictable capacity and workload isolation but require capacity management. Flex-like temporary capacity patterns are useful for bursts; autoscaling can absorb spikes but still has configured limits.
  • X vs Y: optimizing SQL/partitioning reduces bytes scanned and slot use; buying more slots treats capacity pressure without fixing waste. Use JOBS_BY_PROJECT to identify waste before increasing commitments.
  • Exam trap: "queries are queued while slot utilization is saturated" points to reservation capacity or assignment changes. "Queries scanned too much data" points to partitioning, clustering, pruning, or query rewrite.

read_content.heading: BigQuery Query Cost and Failure Patterns
read_content.body:
Troubleshooting BigQuery includes decoding failures and cost symptoms. The exam often gives an error phrase or a business complaint and expects the operational next step.
- Common signals: "Resources exceeded" can mean query memory/shuffle pressure; "Quota exceeded" can mean API, concurrent jobs, streaming, or slot limits; "Access Denied" is IAM or authorized view/policy issue; "not found: table" can be location/project mismatch.
- When to use: dry runs estimate bytes processed before execution; maximum bytes billed prevents runaway ad hoc queries; labels attribute jobs; partition filters reduce scans; materialized views/cache reduce repeated computation when valid.
- Trade-offs: canceling the job stops immediate waste, but durable prevention comes from IAM, reservations, query controls, table design, and scheduled reporting over INFORMATION_SCHEMA. Audit logs can prove who ran a query, but JOBS views are easier for performance and cost fields.
- Exam trap: "reduce future cost with minimal user disruption" is not deleting data or blocking all analysts. Prefer partitioned/clustered tables, authorized views, row/column policies, BI Engine/materialized views where appropriate, and quotas/max bytes billed for guardrails.
read_content.body_html:
Troubleshooting BigQuery includes decoding failures and cost symptoms. The exam often gives an error phrase or a business complaint and expects the operational next step.

  • Common signals: "Resources exceeded" can mean query memory/shuffle pressure; "Quota exceeded" can mean API, concurrent jobs, streaming, or slot limits; "Access Denied" is IAM or authorized view/policy issue; "not found: table" can be location/project mismatch.
  • When to use: dry runs estimate bytes processed before execution; maximum bytes billed prevents runaway ad hoc queries; labels attribute jobs; partition filters reduce scans; materialized views/cache reduce repeated computation when valid.
  • Trade-offs: canceling the job stops immediate waste, but durable prevention comes from IAM, reservations, query controls, table design, and scheduled reporting over INFORMATION_SCHEMA. Audit logs can prove who ran a query, but JOBS views are easier for performance and cost fields.
  • Exam trap: "reduce future cost with minimal user disruption" is not deleting data or blocking all analysts. Prefer partitioned/clustered tables, authorized views, row/column policies, BI Engine/materialized views where appropriate, and quotas/max bytes billed for guardrails.

read_content.heading: Pub/Sub Backlog and Subscriber Health
read_content.body:
Pub/Sub monitoring centers on whether messages are being published, delivered, acknowledged, and retained before expiration. The two exam clue metrics are subscription/oldest_unacked_message_age and subscription/num_undelivered_messages.
- When to use: if backlog grows, subscribers are too slow, down, flow-controlled, failing acks, or blocked by downstream sinks. Alert on oldest unacked message age when freshness matters; alert on undelivered message count when queue depth matters.
- Trade-offs: increasing subscriber instances helps only if the downstream system can absorb the load. If Dataflow reads from Pub/Sub and writes to BigQuery, the bottleneck may be BigQuery Storage Write API quota or transform CPU, not Pub/Sub itself.
- Numeric anchors: unacknowledged messages are retained for the subscription retention window; default topic message retention is measured in days, but exam questions usually care about age approaching the retention deadline rather than exact storage accounting.
- Exam trap: publisher throughput metrics do not prove consumers are healthy. "Messages are accumulating" points to subscription backlog, ack deadline/extensions, dead-letter topics, subscriber errors, and downstream backpressure.
read_content.body_html:
Pub/Sub monitoring centers on whether messages are being published, delivered, acknowledged, and retained before expiration. The two exam clue metrics are subscription/oldest_unacked_message_age and subscription/num_undelivered_messages.

  • When to use: if backlog grows, subscribers are too slow, down, flow-controlled, failing acks, or blocked by downstream sinks. Alert on oldest unacked message age when freshness matters; alert on undelivered message count when queue depth matters.
  • Trade-offs: increasing subscriber instances helps only if the downstream system can absorb the load. If Dataflow reads from Pub/Sub and writes to BigQuery, the bottleneck may be BigQuery Storage Write API quota or transform CPU, not Pub/Sub itself.
  • Numeric anchors: unacknowledged messages are retained for the subscription retention window; default topic message retention is measured in days, but exam questions usually care about age approaching the retention deadline rather than exact storage accounting.
  • Exam trap: publisher throughput metrics do not prove consumers are healthy. "Messages are accumulating" points to subscription backlog, ack deadline/extensions, dead-letter topics, subscriber errors, and downstream backpressure.

read_content.heading: Dataflow Streaming Lag and Watermarks
read_content.body:
Dataflow streaming diagnosis starts with job metrics and the graph. The PDE exam expects system_lag, data_freshness, watermark position, per-stage backlog, worker errors, and sink throughput before generic VM metrics.
- When to use: system_lag rising means elements are waiting in the pipeline; data_freshness rising means output is stale relative to event time; a stuck watermark suggests late data, timers, unbounded side inputs, or a stage that cannot make progress. Inspect the Dataflow job graph to find the hot stage.
- Trade-offs: increasing max workers fixes parallelizable CPU-bound work but not a single hot key, external API limit, BigQuery quota, or sequential transform. Streaming Engine can reduce worker resource pressure but does not remove bad keys or sink limits.
- X vs Y: Pub/Sub oldest_unacked_message_age diagnoses source backlog; Dataflow system_lag diagnoses pipeline processing delay; BigQuery write errors diagnose sink rejection.
- Exam trap: worker CPU at 40% does not mean the pipeline is healthy. A single hot key, GroupByKey, windowing issue, or sink throttling can stall progress while average CPU looks fine.
read_content.body_html:
Dataflow streaming diagnosis starts with job metrics and the graph. The PDE exam expects system_lag, data_freshness, watermark position, per-stage backlog, worker errors, and sink throughput before generic VM metrics.

  • When to use: system_lag rising means elements are waiting in the pipeline; data_freshness rising means output is stale relative to event time; a stuck watermark suggests late data, timers, unbounded side inputs, or a stage that cannot make progress. Inspect the Dataflow job graph to find the hot stage.
  • Trade-offs: increasing max workers fixes parallelizable CPU-bound work but not a single hot key, external API limit, BigQuery quota, or sequential transform. Streaming Engine can reduce worker resource pressure but does not remove bad keys or sink limits.
  • X vs Y: Pub/Sub oldest_unacked_message_age diagnoses source backlog; Dataflow system_lag diagnoses pipeline processing delay; BigQuery write errors diagnose sink rejection.
  • Exam trap: worker CPU at 40% does not mean the pipeline is healthy. A single hot key, GroupByKey, windowing issue, or sink throttling can stall progress while average CPU looks fine.

read_content.heading: Dataflow Batch and Worker Failures
read_content.body:
Batch Dataflow troubleshooting uses job state, worker logs, step-level metrics, and failed element handling. The scenario usually mentions a job stuck, repeatedly retrying, failing after a code change, or producing partial output.
- When to use: inspect worker logs in Cloud Logging for exceptions, dependency failures, serialization issues, out-of-memory, permission denials, and bad records. Use Dataflow counters/distributions to verify records read, records written, and filtered/error counts.
- Trade-offs: retries are useful for transient failures but can hide poison records. Dead-letter outputs, side outputs, or error tables are the correct pattern when individual bad records should not fail the entire pipeline.
- Operational fixes: increase worker machine type or memory for true OOM, increase max workers for parallelizable backlog, fix IAM/service account for access errors, and improve transforms or hot-key fanout for skew.
- Exam trap: "Resource exhausted" is not always a quota increase. It may be worker memory, shuffle, sink throughput, regional capacity, or autoscaling max workers. The right answer inspects stage logs/metrics before making a blind infrastructure change.
read_content.body_html:
Batch Dataflow troubleshooting uses job state, worker logs, step-level metrics, and failed element handling. The scenario usually mentions a job stuck, repeatedly retrying, failing after a code change, or producing partial output.

  • When to use: inspect worker logs in Cloud Logging for exceptions, dependency failures, serialization issues, out-of-memory, permission denials, and bad records. Use Dataflow counters/distributions to verify records read, records written, and filtered/error counts.
  • Trade-offs: retries are useful for transient failures but can hide poison records. Dead-letter outputs, side outputs, or error tables are the correct pattern when individual bad records should not fail the entire pipeline.
  • Operational fixes: increase worker machine type or memory for true OOM, increase max workers for parallelizable backlog, fix IAM/service account for access errors, and improve transforms or hot-key fanout for skew.
  • Exam trap: "Resource exhausted" is not always a quota increase. It may be worker memory, shuffle, sink throughput, regional capacity, or autoscaling max workers. The right answer inspects stage logs/metrics before making a blind infrastructure change.

read_content.heading: Bigtable Latency and Hotspot Diagnosis
read_content.body:
Bigtable troubleshooting is dominated by distribution. The exam often describes low average cluster CPU but high tail latency, one tenant causing slow requests, sequential row keys, timestamp prefixes, or a bright stripe in a heatmap.
- When to use: use Bigtable Key Visualizer to inspect read/write heat by row-key range over time. Use Cloud Monitoring for node CPU, storage utilization, read/write latency, request count, and replication metrics.
- Trade-offs: adding nodes can relieve overall CPU pressure, but it does not fix a hot tablet caused by monotonically increasing keys or a single prefix. Proper row-key design spreads reads and writes across tablets; salting, hashing, reversing timestamps, or prefix changes can help depending on access pattern.
- X vs Y: Key Visualizer diagnoses key distribution; Cloud Monitoring node CPU diagnoses cluster resource saturation; Cloud Logging/audit logs help with access and admin changes.
- Numeric anchor: keep per-node CPU below recommended operational targets for the workload and avoid tablets whose traffic is concentrated in one narrow key range.
- Exam trap: "sequential timestamp row key" plus write latency means redesign row keys; do not choose Cloud SQL indexes or BigQuery partitioning.
read_content.body_html:
