The PDE exam tends to describe incidents as business constraints instead of naming services. Translate the symptom into the control. If a streaming job deploy must not lose data, drain. If a customer table was deleted yesterday, use Cloud SQL PITR or BigQuery time travel depending on the store. If an analytics table must be preserved before a risky transformation, create a BigQuery snapshot. If a region outage must not take down a relational app, use Spanner multi-region or an explicitly planned cross-region database architecture.

RTO/RPO language is decisive

  • RTO seconds/minutes: HA, automatic failover, multi-region managed service.
  • RPO near zero for infrastructure failure: synchronous/regional HA or strongly consistent multi-region service.
  • RPO to exact timestamp after human error: PITR or time travel.
  • RPO longer than built-in short window: explicit snapshots/backups/exports.

Operational awareness language is also decisive

  • Notify on failure: Cloud Monitoring alerting.
  • Find who changed it: Cloud Audit Logs.
  • Group exceptions: Error Reporting.
  • Investigate latency path: Cloud Trace.
  • Analyze job metadata: BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA or service run history.

Trap pattern: avoid answers that only detect a failure when the requirement says mitigate impact, and avoid answers that only recover data when the requirement says automatic failover.

choose_this_when: Cloud Monitoring alerting - requirement says notify on failure, page on-call, SLO burn-rate, uptime check, dashboard, backlog threshold, or metric threshold.
Log-based metric plus Cloud Monitoring alert - failure signal exists only in logs but needs metric-style alerting.
Cloud Logging sink to BigQuery - requirement says analyze logs with SQL, retain operational logs centrally, or correlate failures over time.
Cloud Audit Logs - requirement says determine who deleted, changed IAM, modified a dataset, started a job, or changed bucket policy.
Error Reporting - requirement says group application exceptions, identify first occurrence of a new stack trace, or track crash frequency.
Cloud Trace - requirement says end-to-end latency, slow distributed request path, or dependency span analysis.
Dataflow drain - streaming pipeline must stop or update gracefully with no in-flight data loss and windows completed.
Dataflow cancel - job is broken, corrupting output, runaway cost, or must stop immediately and buffered data loss is acceptable.
Pub/Sub - producers and consumers need decoupling, replayable ingestion, backlog absorption, dead-letter handling, or subscriber failure tolerance.
BigQuery time travel - recover recent BigQuery table state within the configured 2-7 day window.
BigQuery table snapshot - preserve a point-in-time warehouse state before risky change or beyond time travel retention.
Cloud SQL HA - MySQL/PostgreSQL/SQL Server needs automatic zonal failover in the same region.
Cloud SQL PITR - relational database must be restored to the second before a bad write or drop.
Cloud SQL cross-region read replica - regional DR or remote read scale is required and manual promotion is acceptable.
Spanner multi-region - requirement says relational, strong consistency, global/regional outage tolerance, or 99.999% availability.
Cloud Storage soft delete - accidental object or bucket deletion must be recoverable for a short bounded window.
Cloud Storage Object Versioning - overwritten/deleted object generations must be retained until lifecycle cleanup.
Bigtable replication - low-latency serving store needs higher availability or locality across clusters/regions.
avoid_this_when: Dataflow cancel - requirement says no data loss, finish buffered records, preserve windows, or graceful streaming update.
Dataflow drain - pipeline is actively writing corrupt data and must stop immediately.
Cloud Monitoring alone - requirement is to restore deleted data or undo logical corruption.
Error Reporting - signal is infrastructure health, BigQuery slot pressure, Pub/Sub backlog, or database replica lag.
Cloud Logging manual search - requirement says immediate notification or SLO alerting.
BigQuery time travel - recovery point is outside the configured time travel window or must be retained for months.
BigQuery snapshot - source is Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage, Spanner, Bigtable, or another non-BigQuery system.
Cloud SQL HA - problem is a bad DELETE/UPDATE; use PITR or restore, not failover.
Cloud SQL cross-region replica - requirement says automatic zonal HA with same endpoint and low RTO inside one region.
Read replica - question says logical corruption unless replication lag or delayed replica is intentionally part of the design.
Spanner multi-region - requirement is lowest write latency in one region and 99.99% is enough.
Cloud Storage lifecycle rule - requirement is recovery from deletion; lifecycle deletes or transitions data.
Memorystore for Redis - data is authoritative and cannot be reconstructed from a database or event log.
Pub/Sub - requirement is long-term backup, ad hoc SQL analytics, or object/file storage retention.
common_gotchas: Cancel loses in-flight data; never the answer when fidelity matters.
common_traps: Choosing Dataflow cancel for a planned streaming update when the giveaway phrase is no data loss, finish buffered records, or graceful shutdown.
Choosing Dataflow drain when the pipeline is actively corrupting downstream tables and the question prioritizes stopping damage immediately.
Treating Cloud Monitoring dashboards as a restore mechanism; dashboards and alerts detect, backups and PITR recover.
Using Error Reporting for Pub/Sub backlog, BigQuery slot exhaustion, or Cloud SQL disk pressure; those are metric alerts in Cloud Monitoring.
Using manual log review when the requirement says immediate alert, page, or notify on-call.
Assuming BigQuery time travel can recover any historical point; it is limited to the configured 2-7 day window.
Forgetting BigQuery fail-safe is not normal user-queryable time travel; do not rely on it for app recovery design.
Picking BigQuery export scripts over native time travel or snapshots for routine warehouse rollback.
Failing over Cloud SQL to fix a bad DELETE; HA keeps availability but preserves the corrupted state.
Treating a Cloud SQL cross-region replica as automatic same-region HA; it is asynchronous DR/read scale and needs promotion planning.
Assuming a read replica is a backup; replicas can replay bad writes and deletes.
Choosing Spanner multi-region only because data volume is high; Bigtable or BigQuery may fit large data, while Spanner is for relational strong-consistency transactions.
Choosing regional Spanner when the prompt explicitly says 99.999% or survive regional outage.
Using Cloud Storage lifecycle rules as data protection; lifecycle rules delete or transition data and can increase risk if misconfigured.
Using Object Versioning without lifecycle controls on high-churn buckets; it can create cost growth even though it improves recoverability.
Using Pub/Sub as months-long backup storage; it is a messaging buffer and replay mechanism within retention, not archive storage after long periods.

clue_words.phrase: notify on-call when error rate exceeds threshold
SLO burn rate consumed too quickly
centralize logs and query with SQL
who deleted the table or changed IAM
new stack trace grouped automatically
find slow span across services
continuous CPU and memory hot spots
streaming update without losing buffered records
job is corrupting output and must stop now
subscriber down but producers continue publishing
replay failed messages to a subscription
poison messages after repeated failures
recover dropped BigQuery table from yesterday
preserve table before risky migration
recover BigQuery state from 30 days ago
clue_words.answer: BigQuery snapshot if created; otherwise backup/export strategy

clue_words.phrase: restore MySQL to 10:37 AM before bad update
automatic failover for PostgreSQL zone outage
regional database disaster recovery with manual promotion
globally consistent relational database with 99.999%
recover deleted object in bucket within 7 days

decision_table.if_question_says: Update streaming pipeline without losing data

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need to alert when pipeline failure rate, latency, backlog, or resource usage crosses a threshold
Need to alert from a specific log message pattern
service_decision_table.answer: Cloud Logging log-based metric plus Cloud Monitoring alert

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Need centralized searchable logs and long-term SQL analysis of logs
Need to identify who deleted a dataset, changed IAM, or modified a bucket policy
Need grouped stack traces and first-seen application exceptions
Need to find which service hop causes request latency
Need low-overhead CPU/memory profiling in production
Update a streaming Dataflow pipeline without losing buffered/in-flight data
Stop a broken Dataflow job immediately even if buffered data may be lost
service_decision_table.answer: Dataflow cancel or force cancel after normal cancel attempt

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Producers must continue while consumers are down and messages need replay
service_decision_table.answer: Pub/Sub with durable subscription retention and idempotent subscribers

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Messages repeatedly fail due to poison payloads
service_decision_table.answer: Pub/Sub dead-letter topic or quarantine path

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Recover BigQuery table state from yesterday
Preserve BigQuery table before migration or keep point-in-time state beyond time travel
Create writable low-cost copy of BigQuery table for testing
Restore Cloud SQL to the second before a bad transaction
Cloud SQL must survive zone failure automatically in the same region
Cloud SQL needs regional disaster recovery or remote read scaling
service_decision_table.answer: Cloud SQL cross-region read replica with promotion plan

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Relational database must survive regional outage with strong consistency and 99.999% availability
Relational database needs low write latency in one region and high availability but not regional DR
service_decision_table.answer: Spanner regional or Cloud SQL HA depending on scale and relational requirements

service_decision_table.if_question_says: Recover accidentally deleted Cloud Storage object or bucket within short window
Recover overwritten Cloud Storage object generations until lifecycle cleanup
Prevent Cloud Storage objects from being deleted before compliance period ends
service_decision_table.answer: Cloud Storage retention policy, optionally Bucket Lock if irreversible enforcement is required

scenario_patterns.how_question_disguises_it: A team needs to replace a streaming Dataflow job with a new template version during business hours, and the prompt says no in-flight records can be lost or duplicated beyond normal pipeline semantics. The correct move is to drain the old job, start the replacement with a replayable source such as Pub/Sub, and monitor backlog. Cancel is the distractor because it is faster but can discard buffered data.

A database is highly available, but an operator ran a bad UPDATE or dropped a table. The exam offers failover to standby or read replica, but those copies may contain the same logical error. The right answer is Cloud SQL PITR, BigQuery time travel, BigQuery snapshot, Cloud Storage version/soft delete, or a service-specific backup depending on where the data lives.

The story says a zone failed and users must reconnect with minimal interruption, which points to regional HA like Cloud SQL HA. If it says an entire region is unavailable, the answer changes to Spanner multi-region, cross-region replicas with promotion, multi-region/dual-region object storage, or an explicit DR architecture. The hidden axis is failure scope.

The prompt says operations must know within minutes when a data pipeline fails, but answer choices include backups and replicas. Backups recover after failure but do not notify. Pick Cloud Monitoring alerts, log-based metrics, Error Reporting, or workflow notifications, then use recovery controls separately if asked.

A BigQuery table was corrupted recently, so time travel is correct. A table must be restorable after month-end close or after 30 days, so explicit snapshots, exports, or backup policy is required. The giveaway is the recovery timestamp relative to BigQuery time travel's 2-7 day configurable window.

scenario_patterns.pattern: Cache HA mistaken for durable state
scenario_patterns.how_question_disguises_it: A Redis cache must survive node failure with low latency. Memorystore Standard tier or Memorystore for Redis Cluster failover is appropriate. If the wording says the data is authoritative and cannot be recomputed, Redis failover alone is not enough; choose a durable primary store plus cache.

An Airflow or Dataflow task repeatedly fails on the same record, and the answer choices include increasing retries. If the input is poison data, route failures to a dead-letter topic, quarantine table, or error path and alert. Retries help transient outages, not deterministic bad input.

A scenario asks for read scaling and disaster recovery; a replica is reasonable. A scenario asks to undo an accidental delete; a replica is a trap because it can replicate the delete. The correct answer names PITR, backup restore, BigQuery snapshot/time travel, or object version recovery.

The prompt combines regional compliance, low latency, and failure recovery. Co-locate Dataflow and BigQuery/Cloud Storage in the same location for normal processing, but put backups or replicas in permitted alternate regions for DR. Multi-region is only correct when it satisfies both reliability and residency constraints.

The incident is already mitigated, and leadership needs to know who changed a policy, which job wrote bad data, or when errors began. Cloud Audit Logs, Cloud Logging, BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA, and service job histories are the answer, not a new HA configuration.

exam_pattern_notes: If the prompt says no in-flight records can be lost during a streaming Dataflow stop, choose drain; if it says stop corrupt output immediately, choose cancel.
If the prompt says exact timestamp before a bad write, choose PITR/time travel, not HA or replica failover.
If the prompt says zone outage for Cloud SQL, choose HA; if it says region outage, choose cross-region replica/restore plan or a multi-region database service.
If the prompt says 99.999%, global relational, or strong consistency across regions, choose Spanner multi-region.
If the prompt says lowest write latency and users/data are regional, avoid Spanner multi-region unless regional outage tolerance is explicit.
If the prompt says BigQuery rollback within a few days, choose time travel; if beyond the 2-7 day window, choose snapshot/export/backup strategy.
If the prompt says preserve state before risky BigQuery migration, choose table snapshot before running the change.
If the prompt says who changed or deleted a resource, choose Cloud Audit Logs, not Cloud Monitoring.
If the prompt says notify when failures happen, choose Cloud Monitoring alerting, not a dashboard-only or manual log query answer.
If the prompt says exception grouping or first occurrence of new error class, choose Error Reporting; if it says metric threshold, choose Cloud Monitoring.
If the prompt says poison message or repeated processing failure, use dead-letter/quarantine, not unlimited retries.
If the prompt says replay after subscriber outage, Pub/Sub fits; if it says months-long historical recovery, use storage/database backup mechanisms.
If the prompt says Cloud Storage accidental delete, soft delete is direct; Object Versioning is strongest for preserving generations after overwrite/delete.
If the prompt says compliance retention, choose retention policy/Bucket Lock; these prevent deletion rather than simply restoring deleted data later.
final_review_bullets: Awareness equals Cloud Monitoring alerts; evidence equals Cloud Logging and Cloud Audit Logs; grouped exceptions equal Error Reporting.
Dataflow drain is for graceful streaming stop; cancel can drop buffered/in-flight data.
Batch Dataflow jobs are canceled, not drained; drain is a streaming pipeline concept.
BigQuery time travel is automatic, recent, and short-window: default 7 days, configurable 2-7 days.
BigQuery fail-safe is 7 days but not normal user-accessible recovery; use snapshots for planned longer retention.
BigQuery snapshot is read-only point-in-time state with independent expiration.
Cloud SQL HA survives zonal failure; Cloud SQL PITR undoes logical corruption.
Cloud SQL cross-region read replica is asynchronous DR/read scale, not the same as automatic regional HA.
A replica can replicate bad writes; use PITR, snapshot, backup, or versioning for corruption recovery.
Spanner regional favors lower write latency; Spanner multi-region favors 99.999% and regional-outage tolerance.
Cloud Storage soft delete defaults to 7 days on new buckets and can retain 7-90 days.
Cloud Storage Object Versioning keeps noncurrent generations until lifecycle rules remove them.
Retention policy prevents deletion; versioning/soft delete enables restore; lifecycle manages cost.
Pub/Sub absorbs subscriber outages and supports replay within retention, but is not long-term backup.
Dead-letter topics isolate poison messages; retries alone do not fix deterministic bad input.
Bigtable replication improves serving availability; Bigtable backups restore from accidental mutation/deletion.
